A: You need to keep a person warm for 6 hours with no electricity. You only have a brick, a blanket, direct sunlight, and a thermometer. Design a method to keep the skin-side temperature between 30–35°C without causing burns.
D: Wrap the person in the blanket
Use the brick as a weight to hold the blanket in place
Warm the brick in the sun and place it near the person
Add extra blanket layers for more insulation
Move the brick away if it feels too hot
Check temperature periodically with the thermometer
Place the brick at the feet for warmth
Avoid direct contact between hot brick and skin

B.rule: Treat the setup as a thermal RC system with a safety constraint: brick = heat capacity, blanket = thermal resistance, and the control action is when/where to couple or decouple heat flow to keep surface temperature in-range.
B.unlocks: A controllable heat-release schedule (charge/hold/discharge) while explicitly respecting a maximum safe contact temperature.
B.justification: Once you model heat flow and safety limits, you can time and position coupling to shape the temperature curve instead of just ‘adding warmth’.

C: Sun-heat the brick, then wrap it in multiple blanket folds to create a thick insulating sleeve; place it near (not directly on) the body, and use the thermometer to tune distance and insulation thickness until the skin-side stays 30–35°C. When temperature trends down, briefly increase coupling by moving the brick closer or reducing one fold; when it trends up, increase insulation or distance. This turns the brick+blanket into a tunable heat buffer that avoids burns while sustaining warmth for hours.

A: How might we enable LLMs to spend more output tokens to get predictably better results?

B.rule: Treat an answer as a set of atomic claims with measurable residuals; tokens buy residual reduction.
B.unlocks: A control loop that allocates token budget to the largest failing residual (math, constraints, citations, consistency).
B.justification: Extra steps help only when they systematically shrink claim-level error signals rather than add prose.

C: Generate a draft, extract it into atomic claims, score each claim on residuals (constraint violations, arithmetic checks, source support), then spend additional tokens only on the highest-residual claims until all residuals fall below thresholds.

A: Propose a solution to Los Angeles traffic.

B.rule: Treat commutes as packets on a congested network: delay explodes past a capacity threshold.
B.unlocks: Small peak-load shaping (credits, scheduling, batching) that yields outsized latency reduction.
B.justification: In queueing networks, reducing utilization near saturation produces superlinear travel-time gains.

C: Issue tradable peak-hour corridor credits to employers and drivers: peak trips spend credits, off-peak earns credits; integrate with payroll/HR scheduling so companies can “batch” commutes into less-saturated windows and get reliable travel-time drops without blanket tolling.

A: Design an original sport that combines elements of three existing sports.

B.rule: Treat a sport as modular primitives: locomotion mode + possession rule + scoring geometry.
B.unlocks: Novel sports by swapping exactly one primitive from each parent sport.
B.justification: Most “new sports” fail because too many primitives change; modular swaps preserve learnability and tension.

C: Create “Skyline Relay”: parkour locomotion through obstacles, ultimate-style passing (no running with the disc), and volleyball scoring geometry (must volley over a net into zones); the primitives snap together cleanly, making strategy about route choice, passing windows, and zone selection.

A: What could be a novel use for blockchain technology outside of cryptocurrency?

B.rule: Treat blockchain as a multi-party, append-only event log with replayable state transitions.
B.unlocks: ‘What happened when’ reconstruction across organizations without a single trusted operator.
B.justification: If each actor signs events, anyone can replay the log to audit process integrity without seeing proprietary payloads.

C: Build “regulatory replay ledgers” for AI/biotech compliance: labs append signed checkpoints (protocol version, safety checks passed, data lineage hashes), auditors replay the log to verify procedure adherence, and sensitive content stays off-chain behind hash commitments.

A: What might be an unexpected solution to reducing plastic waste in oceans?

B.rule: Treat plastic leakage as a missing reverse-logistics channel with no profitable backhaul.
B.unlocks: Turn retrieval into a commodity flow by attaching standardized bounties and using return legs of ships.
B.justification: Waste persists when collection is economically negative; paying for sorted returns creates an incentive-compatible loop.

C: Create port-side “reverse-vending depots” that pay standardized bounties for sorted plastic; fund via packaging insurance and use fishing boats/cargo ships as backhaul carriers on return trips, making ocean retrieval part of existing logistics rather than a separate cleanup industry.

A: How might we design a city that functions entirely underwater?

B.rule: Treat the city as a fault-tolerant system of dockable pressure modules connected by umbilicals.
B.unlocks: Isolation boundaries, hot-swappable neighborhoods, and scalable expansion via standardized interfaces.
B.justification: Underwater safety is dominated by containment and maintenance; modular vessels localize failures and simplify upgrades.

C: Build standardized “reef pods” (pressure-rated modules) that dock to a service spine carrying power/data/water; pods can detach for repair, farms occupy light-optimized pods at the periphery, and vertical ballast elevators provide transport between depth layers with minimal hull penetrations.

A: I have a fleet of 100 drones, how can I use them?

B.rule: Treat the fleet as a distributed sensing mesh with coverage, redundancy, and handoff schedules.
B.unlocks: Persistent monitoring by rotating duty cycles, plus rapid local response via swarming.
B.justification: Parallel agents can maintain continuous coverage and reassign tasks dynamically when uncertainty spikes.

C: Offer “pop-up situational awareness”: drones run rotating shifts to keep continuous mapping of an area, auto-trigger denser sampling when anomalies appear (fire edge, flood rise, damage), and form ad-hoc relays so data keeps flowing even when infrastructure is down.

A: Describe a sustainable farming method that could be used in a floating city.

B.rule: Treat the city as a closed-loop nutrient ledger: every waste stream is an input stream.
B.unlocks: Coupling wastewater polishing to food production with measurable mass-balance constraints.
B.justification: Floating systems are supply-constrained; closing nitrogen/phosphorus/water loops reduces imports and increases resilience.

C: Deploy modular aquaponic loops: fish waste feeds algae/bioreactors that nitrify water, plants grow in stacked channels, and biochar/struvite capture recover phosphorus; the farm doubles as wastewater treatment, producing food while stabilizing water quality.

A: Design a smart-clothing line that survives weekly washing for 2 years and remains upgradeable. Constraints: electronics must detach in under 30 seconds, the garment must look like normal clothing, and the added bill of materials cost must be under $20 per item.
D: Add fitness sensors to a shirt
Put a charging pocket in the garment

B.rule: Treat smart clothing as a modular interface layer under lifecycle constraints: textile = durable aesthetic shell; electronics = standardized, swappable liner that meets time-to-detach and wash-cycle requirements.
B.unlocks: Upgradeable sensing/actuation without sacrificing washability, comfort, or garment longevity—because failure domains are separated.
B.justification: Most wearables fail when electronics are inseparable from textiles; modular separation matches how garments actually age and get cleaned.

C: Create a ‘core + liner’ system: each garment is a normal-looking outer shell with a single standardized docking seam (snaps + pogo pins) for a thin removable liner that contains sensors, battery, and compute. The liner pops out in under 30 seconds for washing and can be upgraded without replacing the garment, while the shell stays timeless and durable. Keep cost under $20 by sharing the same liner across multiple items and limiting onboard components to a few high-value sensors plus a low-power MCU. It works because the wash/aging constraints apply to fabric, while upgrade constraints apply to electronics—modularity satisfies both.

A: Propose a system for energy-harvesting from natural disasters.

B.rule: Treat disasters as intermittent high-power flows where infrastructure must be sacrificial and modular.
B.unlocks: Clip-on harvesters with safe failure modes, rapid deployment, and replaceable layers.
B.justification: Rigid permanent systems get destroyed; sacrificial modules can extract energy without catastrophic loss.

C: Install anchor points along flood channels and deploy “harvest mats” with debris-tolerant microturbines before storms; they generate into local storage, then shear off safely past load limits, allowing recovery/replacement instead of total infrastructure failure.

A: How might the education system be revolutionized by virtual reality classrooms?

B.rule: Treat VR as an instrumented practice environment: resettable worlds + measurable skills + feedback loops.
B.unlocks: Deliberate practice with replay, adaptive difficulty, and performance traces for coaching.
B.justification: Learning accelerates when tasks can be repeated safely and assessed continuously, not only explained.

C: Turn classes into VR practice labs: students repeatedly run experiments, negotiations, or builds with instant feedback and replay; teachers coach using telemetry (errors, timing, decision paths), and curricula become sequences of measurable skill missions rather than lectures.

A: Design a mobile app that increases verified acts of kindness by 20% in 8 weeks without public posting. Constraints: each user spends ≤30 seconds/day, and the system must minimize gaming (fake reports).

B.rule: Treat kindness as a network process with incentives and verification: actions propagate through local edges, but the system must use low-friction, privacy-preserving confirmation signals to prevent reward hacking.
B.unlocks: Micro-missions + reciprocal confirmation + lightweight audits that create measurable lift while staying private and fast.
B.justification: Behavior spreads when actions are tiny and reinforced, but metrics only improve reliably if rewards are tied to signals that are hard to fake.

C: Give users 30-second ‘micro-missions’ tailored to context (thank-you note, quick help, small courtesy), and require a privacy-preserving confirmation: either the recipient taps a one-time QR/NFC token, or the app uses paired “gratitude receipts” that only unlock rewards when two independent parties confirm. Add small random audits (e.g., brief follow-up prompts) to deter gaming without heavy friction. Visualize impact as anonymized neighborhood ripples rather than public posts. It works because it couples contagion-style reinforcement with verification signals, so the metric reflects real actions rather than self-reporting.

A: Design a spectator-friendly non-physical professional sport. Constraints: matches last 20 minutes, the score updates at least once per minute on average, a new viewer must understand the objective within 60 seconds, and cheating must be detectable.

B.rule: Treat sport as visible skill under pressure with legible state, frequent score deltas, and auditability; engineer the game so actions cause immediate, explainable scoring changes and leave verifiable traces.
B.unlocks: Time-boxed phases, drafts, and scoring tied to observable moves plus automated integrity checks—so spectators get momentum and fairness.
B.justification: Non-physical sports succeed when objectives are instantly legible and outcomes track decision quality in real time, with credible anti-cheat.

C: Create a ‘Live Systems League’: teams draft constraints (budget, latency, risk) then solve short optimization rounds where each submission is scored immediately on efficiency/robustness/explainability, producing frequent point swings. The objective is explained as ‘build the best system under changing constraints,’ and the scoreboard updates each round (every 60–90 seconds). Cheating is detectable because submissions must be reproducible in a sandbox with logged traces and automated validators. This works by designing for legibility (simple objective), momentum (frequent deltas), and integrity (auditable execution).

A: Devise a farming technique to harvest dinoflagellates and retain their bioluminescence.

B.rule: Treat glow as a controllable stimulus-response separate from biomass handling; shear is the main kill-switch.
B.unlocks: Decouple growth/harvest from stimulation using low-shear transport and timed triggering.
B.justification: Cells die from mechanical stress; you can preserve viability by minimizing shear and triggering glow only at use-time.

C: Grow in low-shear wave reactors, harvest via laminar overflow into dark temperature-stable buffers, and trigger bioluminescence only at display time using brief, gentle mechanical pulses—keeping harvesting and stimulation as separate “modes.”

A: How can we reduce hallucinations in LLM-generated summaries without sacrificing fluency?

B.rule: Treat summarization as lossy compression with a per-claim provenance constraint.
B.unlocks: Claim extraction → source alignment → regenerate only from aligned claims.
B.justification: Hallucinations occur when the compressor invents missing bits; enforcing provenance removes invention pressure.

C: Draft a summary, extract atomic claims, align each claim to a specific source span (or delete/hedge it), then regenerate a fluent summary constrained to the aligned claim set—so fluency is preserved but unsupported content cannot survive.

A: Design a low-cost way to keep vaccines cold in rural areas with unreliable electricity.

B.rule: Treat cold storage as time-shifted heat flow buffered by phase-change capacity (a ‘cold battery’).
B.unlocks: Predictable charge/discharge cycles, passive hold time, and swap-based distribution.
B.justification: Phase-change materials store large cooling energy near target temperatures, bridging outages reliably.

C: Use PCM-lined vaccine carriers that charge during available power/solar windows and passively hold 2–8°C for days; add a simple indicator and a swap network so carriers rotate through charge points like rechargeable batteries across clinics.

A: How might we make urban intersections safer for cyclists without slowing buses too much?

B.rule: Treat the intersection as a conflict matrix; safety improves by eliminating overlap in high-risk movement pairs.
B.unlocks: Micro-phasing (early release), protected turns, and geometry that reduces ambiguous merges while preserving bus priority.
B.justification: Crashes cluster where permissions overlap; short protected phases can remove conflicts with minimal throughput loss.

C: Give cyclists an early-release micro-phase to clear the conflict zone, then grant buses extended green via signal priority; add a hardened corner island to slow turn speeds and physically prevent right-hook conflicts without increasing bus delay.

A: Propose a novel way to teach kids fractions that avoids worksheets.

B.rule: Treat fractions as invariants under reversible transformations: cut/fold/recombine without changing total value.
B.unlocks: Equivalence discovery through legal moves, like a conservation law game.
B.justification: Understanding locks in when kids experience ‘same amount’ surviving shape changes before seeing symbols.

C: Run a “fraction trading market”: kids get paper wholes, perform allowed transformations (folds/cuts), and can trade only if they can recombine pieces to match a reference whole; after trading, labels (1/2, 2/4) are introduced as names for the invariant.

A: How can a small restaurant reduce food waste while keeping menu creativity?

B.rule: Treat the menu as a directed graph: ingredients → components → dishes, with rerouting when nodes overflow.
B.unlocks: Modular prep libraries, surplus-driven routing, and creativity via recombination rather than new inventory.
B.justification: Waste falls when surplus ingredients have multiple outgoing edges into appealing dishes.

C: Create a prep component library (bases/sauces/pickles/roasts) and a daily algorithm: identify highest-surplus ingredients, route them through components into 2 rotating specials, and add one ‘fresh accent’ to keep novelty while systematically draining surplus.

A: Design a fair system to allocate scarce GPU time across teams in a company.

B.rule: Treat GPU time as bandwidth: jobs are flows, teams are tenants, the cluster is a router with congestion control.
B.unlocks: Credit-based rate limiting, burst borrowing, and QoS floors with transparent incentives.
B.justification: Networking has mature fairness mechanisms; mapping jobs→flows enables predictable, enforceable allocation.

C: Give each team a weekly credit refill (QoS floor) and let jobs bid credits per GPU-hour; allow limited burst by borrowing future credits and reward early termination with rebates—so the scheduler acts like congestion control, balancing fairness and urgency.

A: How might we detect deepfake audio in real-time during customer support calls?

B.rule: Treat voice as two coupled channels: signal physics (prosody/timing) + semantic intent; deepfakes decouple them.
B.unlocks: Live challenge-response that stresses timing/prosody plus artifact scoring under constrained repetition.
B.justification: Synthetic audio often fails on micro-timing continuity and rapid constraint-following without glitches.

C: Insert a low-friction voice challenge: ask the caller to repeat a randomized phrase with a changing rhythm or emphasis; score micro-prosody continuity and spectral artifacts in real time, and gate high-risk actions until the combined physics+semantic score passes.

A: Propose a way to make apartment buildings quieter without major renovations.

B.rule: Treat noise as energy traveling through a few dominant transmission paths (gaps, vents, doors, flanking).
B.unlocks: Chokepoint damping: seal/decouple the highest-coupling interfaces first, guided by quick impulse tests.
B.justification: Perceived leakage is often dominated by a small number of high-coupling interfaces, not total wall mass.

C: Use a phone-based impulse test (clap/sweep) to identify the dominant path per room, then apply a removable ‘quiet kit’ targeted to that path: magnetic door gaskets, vent baffles that keep airflow, and corner traps—maximizing reduction without construction.

A: How could we make scientific peer review faster while improving reliability?

B.rule: Treat a paper as a dependency graph of claims; each node has an evidence/test obligation.
B.unlocks: Automated checks on easy nodes, then route only fragile/high-impact nodes to specialists.
B.justification: Review time should concentrate where validity hinges; claim-graphs expose which nodes dominate correctness.

C: Require a ‘claims table’ and build the claim dependency graph; run automated checks (stats consistency, code/data reproducibility, plagiarism) on eligible nodes, then assign expert reviewers only to the few nodes with high impact and weak evidence, speeding review while raising rigor.

A: Design a playful intervention that increases office water intake without notifications and without collecting personal health data. Constraint: average additional time cost must be ≤10 seconds per person per hour.
D: Set a schedule to drink water

B.rule: Treat hydration as a micro-habit loop under privacy and interruption constraints: tiny action + immediate feedback + optional group-level reinforcement, with no personal tracking.
B.unlocks: Ambient triggers and shared feedback that make sips salient without pings or individual data.
B.justification: If the action is near-zero friction and the reward is immediate and visible, repetition forms without intrusive reminders.

C: Place a shared ‘sip beacon’ per area: after taking a sip, anyone can tap a nearby physical token (NFC puck) that advances a communal display (e.g., a plant growing) with no identity attached. The only logged data is aggregate taps per zone per hour, keeping privacy intact. Because tapping takes ~1 second and requires no app check-ins, it fits the ≤10 seconds/hour constraint. It works by creating an ambient, low-friction reinforcement loop that doesn’t interrupt work or rely on personal tracking.

A: How might we reduce microplastics shed from synthetic clothing during washing?

B.rule: Treat shedding as abrasion-driven surface erosion; emissions scale with shear energy and fiber fatigue.
B.unlocks: Reduce shear (cycle design), reduce friction (conditioning), and capture at the highest concentration zone.
B.justification: Most fiber release occurs under high agitation; lowering abrasion and capturing locally cuts emissions sharply.

C: Add a short pre-conditioning tumble using a rinse-safe friction reducer, switch to a low-shear wash profile, and use a drum-wall baffle insert that traps fibers where concentration peaks; dispose of captured lint as a controlled waste stream.

A: Propose a protocol that improves name recall at networking events with no phone use and no note-taking. Constraints: it must take ≤10 seconds per introduction and should not feel awkward.
D: Connect the name to a face feature

B.rule: Treat a name as a retrieval key that needs multiple independent cues under time/awkwardness constraints; design a deterministic 10-second encoding routine that produces 3 orthogonal cues once.
B.unlocks: High-recall encoding without tools: phonetic confirmation + semantic hook + contextual binding, each lightweight.
B.justification: Recall improves when any one of several distinct cues can trigger retrieval, and a fixed routine removes social friction.

C: Use a fixed 10-second ‘3-cue handshake’: (1) confirm pronunciation (‘Did I say it right?’), (2) get a one-word hook (‘Is that like the word/meaning X?’), and (3) bind to shared context (‘So you’re here for Y’). Then repeat the full name once while making eye contact. It stays non-awkward because each step is conversationally normal and brief. It works because it creates three independent retrieval cues without requiring notes or a phone.

A: Design a low-cost way to reduce queue times at a busy hospital pharmacy by 30% without adding staff. Constraints: no new software purchases, must preserve medication safety checks.

B.rule: Treat the pharmacy as a multi-class queueing system with a bottleneck safety-check stage; the control knobs are class separation (simple vs complex scripts), batching policy (when to group), and WIP limits (how much work enters the bottleneck).
B.unlocks: Fast lane + controlled batching that reduces variability hitting the bottleneck while keeping safety checks intact.
B.justification: Queue delay explodes with utilization and variability; reducing arrival variability and limiting WIP around the bottleneck cuts wait time without increasing capacity.

C: Split prescriptions into two classes at intake using a simple checklist: ‘fast’ (single med, no interactions, no prior auth) and ‘complex’. Run a visible WIP cap for the safety-check station: intake only releases a fixed number of jobs into check; the rest wait upstream. Batch complex scripts into fixed review waves (e.g., every 15 minutes) while fast scripts flow continuously, and pre-stage fast-lane labels/bags. This uses class separation + batching + WIP limits to cut variability at the bottleneck while preserving the same safety-check steps.

A: Propose a way to make office meetings end on time without banning discussion. Constraints: no manager policing, no new tools, and participants must feel heard.

B.rule: Treat a meeting as a bandwidth allocation problem: topics are flows competing for limited time, and the control knobs are token budget per topic, preemption (when to pause), and a ‘spillover channel’ for unresolved items.
B.unlocks: Fair time slicing with graceful preemption so discussion continues—but not inside the time-critical channel.
B.justification: Overruns happen when topics have no enforced budget and no preemption mechanism; adding a spillover channel preserves voice while protecting the clock.

C: Give each agenda item a visible time budget and a ‘pause rule’: when the budget ends, anyone can call “park” and the group must capture the next concrete step plus owner in one sentence. Unfinished debate moves to a defined spillover channel: a 10-minute optional add-on with only the stakeholders, immediately after the meeting. This makes time a shared token budget, enables preemption without hierarchy, and keeps people heard by guaranteeing a recorded next step and a continuation slot.

A: Design a method to prevent mold in a humid apartment bedroom during monsoon season without buying a dehumidifier. Constraints: windows can be opened, and you can rearrange furniture; budget $20.

B.rule: Treat mold risk as a dew-point and surface-temperature problem; the control knobs are airflow path (ventilation), thermal boundary (insulation/spacing), and moisture sources (localized wet loads).
B.unlocks: Prevent condensation at the wall interface by changing airflow and surface conditions rather than ‘killing mold’ after it appears.
B.justification: Mold blooms where surfaces stay above critical humidity; reducing boundary-layer humidity and preventing dew formation breaks the growth condition.

C: Create a ‘dry boundary layer’ behind furniture: pull beds/wardrobes 10–15 cm from exterior walls and open a cross-vent path twice daily (even 10 minutes) to flush humid air. Use $20 on a cheap humidity indicator + two passive absorbers placed at the coldest wall corners, and move any wet loads (laundry, towels) out of the room. If condensation is seen, add a cardboard/foam spacer strip behind the furniture to reduce cold-wall contact and keep air moving. Airflow path + spacing + source control prevents the surface from sitting in a mold-friendly microclimate.

A: Propose a strategy to reduce customer support ticket backlog by 40% in 6 weeks without hiring. Constraints: keep CSAT stable, and no new SaaS tools.

B.rule: Treat support as a defect pipeline: tickets cluster into a few repeatable ‘failure modes’ with different fix costs; the control knobs are deflection rate (self-serve), containment (one-touch resolution), and upstream elimination (fixing root causes).
B.unlocks: Backlog reduction by attacking the Pareto failure modes with a split policy: deflect, contain, or eliminate—depending on mode economics.
B.justification: Backlog persists when every ticket is handled the same way; mode-based control reallocates effort to actions that shrink future arrivals, not just today’s queue.

C: Run a 1-week failure-mode census: tag the top 10 ticket themes and measure (a) repeats/week, (b) avg handle time, (c) engineering dependency. For each theme choose one lever: (1) deflect via a single crisp help-center article + macro link for low-risk questions, (2) contain via a one-touch playbook checklist for medium complexity, (3) eliminate via one engineering fix for the top 2 high-repeat bugs. Freeze new edge-case investigations for 2 weeks unless they’re in the top modes. This uses deflection + containment + elimination knobs to reduce arrivals while keeping CSAT stable.

A: Design a way to improve team decision-making quality when data is uncertain and time is limited. Constraints: decisions must be made within 30 minutes; avoid endless debate.

B.rule: Treat decisions as bets with bounded loss; the control knobs are confidence calibration (what would change my mind), reversible vs irreversible classification, and commitment size (how big a bet now).
B.unlocks: A structured ‘small bet first’ protocol that converts uncertainty into controlled exposure and clear stop conditions.
B.justification: Bad decisions under uncertainty come from uncalibrated confidence and oversized commitments; separating reversibility and sizing the bet reduces regret while keeping speed.

C: In 30 minutes, run a 3-step bet protocol: (1) classify the decision as reversible/irreversible and set a max-loss bound, (2) each person states a ‘change-my-mind’ datum (one thing that would flip their stance), (3) commit to the smallest reversible action that produces that datum within a defined window. If irreversible, commit only after agreeing on the max-loss mitigation (rollback plan, guardrails). This uses calibration + reversibility + bet sizing to avoid debate loops while improving quality.

A: Propose a method to make a crowded subway platform safer during rush hour without building new infrastructure. Constraints: must not reduce train throughput.

B.rule: Treat the platform as a bidirectional pedestrian flow system with conflict points at doors; the control knobs are lane separation (where people stand), phase timing (when boarding vs alighting), and buffer zones (kept clear areas).
B.unlocks: Flow control that reduces collisions at the door interface while keeping boarding speed high.
B.justification: Crowd risk spikes from opposing flows crossing in a small area; separating lanes and phasing reduces conflicts without slowing overall throughput.

C: Paint and enforce a simple ‘door buffer’ geometry: two side lanes for alighting (kept clear) and a central queue lane for boarding. Use a short phase rule communicated by floor markings: first 5 seconds are alight-only into the side lanes; then boarding begins from the central lane. Platform staff aren’t required if the geometry is self-enforcing (clear arrows + footprints) and the buffer is physically indicated with textured tape. Lane separation + micro-phasing + buffer zones reduce crowd conflict without changing train frequency.

A: Design a way to reduce forgotten tasks at home for a couple, without using apps or alarms. Constraints: must feel non-annoying and take under 2 minutes per day.
D: Keep a calendar on the fridge

B.rule: Treat memory as cue-dependent retrieval; the control knobs are cue placement (where it appears), cue specificity (what action it triggers), and decay/reset (when cues disappear).
B.unlocks: A minimal cue system that triggers actions at the right context and self-cleans so it doesn’t become visual noise.
B.justification: People forget not because they lack intent, but because cues are absent at the moment of action; context-locked cues outperform generic lists.

C: Create a two-minute daily ‘cue placement’ ritual: choose up to three tasks and attach a physical cue at the action location (e.g., key-on-top-of-trash-bag, utensil-in-front-of-vitamin bottle, wallet clipped to return package). Use only cues that block the normal flow slightly, and require that a cue must be removed immediately after the task (self-reset). Cue placement + specificity + reset makes tasks hard to miss without becoming annoying.

A: Propose a way to reduce energy use in a large office building by 15% without changing HVAC equipment. Constraints: must not noticeably reduce comfort.

B.rule: Treat the building as a set of thermal zones with occupancy-driven load; the control knobs are scheduling (when zones are conditioned), setpoint deadband (allowed drift), and heat gains (plug loads/lighting) timing.
B.unlocks: Load shaping: reduce conditioning during low-occupancy periods while preserving perceived comfort during occupied windows.
B.justification: Comfort depends on conditions when people are present; tightening control to occupancy and allowing small deadbands saves energy without felt impact.

C: Implement a zone schedule based on real occupancy patterns (badge/room-booking logs or simple observation): condition only the zones used in each hour block and let others drift within a wider deadband. Shift heat gains by timing: default lights and non-critical plug loads off before peak cooling hours (e.g., printers, extra monitors), and consolidate teams into fewer zones on low-occupancy days so fewer zones need tight control. Scheduling + deadband + gain-timing reduces total thermal work while keeping occupied areas comfortable.

A: Design a safe, low-cost way to keep a laptop from overheating during a long video call in a hot room. Constraints: no external fan and no ice; only household items.
D: Put the laptop on a table

B.rule: Treat cooling as convection control: the control knobs are airflow gap height under the laptop, chimney effect (vertical flow path), and thermal contact area (avoid insulating surfaces).
B.unlocks: Passive airflow amplification—turn the laptop into a chimney-cooled device without moving parts.
B.justification: Most overheating is boundary-layer limited; increasing underbody airflow and creating a buoyancy-driven plume increases heat transfer with no fan.

C: Elevate the rear of the laptop 2–4 cm using two identical objects (e.g., bottle caps or erasers) to create a stable airflow gap, and place it over a rigid surface with a central cutout (e.g., a tray or stiff book with a hole) so warm air can rise freely. Ensure the intake vents aren’t blocked and orient the laptop so hot exhaust has a clear upward path (chimney effect). If available, add a folded-paper ‘chimney skirt’ behind the exhaust to guide the buoyant plume upward rather than back toward the intake. Gap height + chimney path + contact control increases passive convection and reduces throttling.

A: Propose a method to improve reliability of a weekly ML training pipeline that often fails due to flaky data dependencies. Constraints: cannot change upstream data producers; must reduce failures by 50% within a month.

B.rule: Treat the pipeline as a contract system with explicit freshness and completeness guarantees; the control knobs are snapshotting (what version you train on), validation gates (what you require before running), and fallback modes (what you do when guarantees fail).
B.unlocks: Turn flaky upstream data into stable training inputs via versioned snapshots + strict gates + graceful degradation.
B.justification: Pipelines fail when they assume live dependencies; introducing stable snapshots and preflight validation converts runtime failures into early, actionable decisions.

C: Create a versioned training snapshot step: at the start of the week, materialize a manifest of required inputs (paths + hashes + row counts) and copy/lock them into a dated snapshot location. Add a preflight gate that checks completeness and schema invariants before any expensive compute begins. If the gate fails, switch to a fallback mode: train on the last good snapshot while emitting a structured report of missing/changed inputs. Snapshotting + gating + fallback removes live flakiness from the training run and cuts failures quickly without touching upstream producers.

A: Design a way to stop a shared office kitchen from running out of clean mugs by 2pm. Constraints: no buying new mugs, no manager enforcement.

B.rule: Treat mug availability as a closed-loop inventory with a return-delay bottleneck; control knobs are friction at return (wash effort), visibility of deficit (stock signal), and a hard WIP cap (limit on dirty accumulation).
B.unlocks: Make the return path easier than the take path, and prevent unbounded dirty backlog.
B.justification: Shortages persist when the return delay is unbounded; limiting dirty WIP and adding immediate stock signals stabilizes the loop.

C: Create a ‘dirty-mug WIP cap’: only N dirty mugs fit in a visible bin next to the sink; if it’s full, you must wash one before taking a clean mug. Add a simple stock signal: a green/red card on the clean shelf that flips to red when fewer than X mugs remain, prompting immediate returns. Reduce return friction by pre-staging soap/sponge and leaving a 20-second rinse protocol on the counter. WIP cap + visibility + lower return friction keeps clean inventory from collapsing mid-day.

A: Propose a method to prevent missed handoffs between shifts in a 24/7 operations team. Constraints: no new software, handoff must take ≤5 minutes.

B.rule: Treat handoff as a state transfer with invariants; control knobs are state compression (only deltas), acknowledgment (explicit receipt), and a bounded queue (limit open items).
B.unlocks: A 5-minute state packet that is complete, acknowledged, and cannot grow without bound.
B.justification: Handoffs fail when state is ambiguous or unacknowledged; a small invariant-based packet with receipt prevents silent drops.

C: Use a fixed ‘3-delta packet’: (1) Top 3 changes since last shift, (2) Top 3 risks with thresholds, (3) Open queue list capped at 10 with owners. The incoming shift must read back one line per risk (acknowledgment) and explicitly accept ownership of each queue item. If the queue exceeds 10, the outgoing shift must either close items or escalate before handoff. Delta compression + readback receipt + bounded queue keeps handoff under 5 minutes and prevents misses.

A: Design a way to cut cafeteria food line time by 25% without adding staff or registers. Constraints: must keep order accuracy.

B.rule: Treat the line as a two-stage system: decision-making and transaction; control knobs are pre-commitment (decide before counter), batch size (group similar orders), and service differentiation (fast lane vs slow lane).
B.unlocks: Remove decision time from the bottleneck and reduce variance at the register.
B.justification: Queueing delay is driven by high-variance service times; separating slow decisions from the transaction stage increases throughput without new staff.

C: Move the menu decision upstream: a ‘choice board’ before the queue with 6–8 combo codes; customers pick a code before reaching the counter. Create two lanes: combo-code lane (fast) and custom-order lane (slow). Batch at the kitchen pass: when three identical codes appear, prepare them together. Pre-commitment + batching + fast/slow differentiation reduces register variance while keeping accuracy (codes map to fixed recipes).

A: Propose a protocol to reduce miscommunication in cross-team Slack threads. Constraints: no new tools, must not increase message volume.

B.rule: Treat a thread as a shared constraint-solving process; control knobs are explicit question type (ask vs propose), commitment level (FYI vs decision), and closure condition (what ends the thread).
B.unlocks: Low-volume clarity by forcing messages to declare intent and exit criteria.
B.justification: Threads sprawl when intent is implicit; lightweight ‘message headers’ reduce back-and-forth without adding volume.

C: Require a one-line prefix on the first message only: [ASK], [PROPOSE], or [DECIDE], plus a closure condition (e.g., ‘Ends when X owner confirms Y’). Replies must either (a) answer the ask, (b) edit the proposal with one concrete change, or (c) state a blocking constraint. When closure condition is met, the opener posts a single ‘CLOSED: decision + owner + next step’ line. Intent + commitment + closure reduces ambiguity without increasing message count.

A: Design a method to keep a room at 22–26°C for 4 hours during a heat wave with no AC. You have: two large plastic water bottles, a towel, a window, and a fan that runs only 15 minutes per hour.
D: Place water bottles in front of fan

B.rule: Treat cooling as a heat-exchanger scheduling problem; control knobs are evaporation rate (towel wetness + airflow), thermal mass charging (cool bottles at night), and airflow path timing (fan duty cycle + window state).
B.unlocks: Time-shift cooling and maximize evaporative efficiency only during fan windows.
B.justification: With limited power, you must synchronize high heat-transfer modes (evaporation + airflow) to the fan duty cycle and use water as a cold buffer.

C: Before the 4-hour period, chill the bottles as much as possible (coolest place available). Each hour, run the fan for 15 minutes aimed across a damp towel draped so air passes through it (not directly onto the towel surface only). Place the bottles behind the towel so incoming air is cooled by both evaporation and the bottle thermal mass. Keep the window closed during fan-on to avoid importing hot air; open it briefly only when outdoor air is cooler than indoor. Evaporation knob (wetness), buffer knob (bottle temp), and timing knob (fan/window schedule) keep temperature in-range as long as humidity allows.

A: Propose a way to prevent silent data drift in a production ML model without adding new monitoring services. Constraints: must detect within 24 hours.

B.rule: Treat drift as distribution mismatch detectable via cheap invariants; control knobs are reference window (what ‘normal’ is), sketch type (histograms/quantiles), and trigger policy (thresholds + persistence).
B.unlocks: In-pipeline drift sentinels using compact sketches rather than new infra.
B.justification: You can detect many drifts with low-cost summary statistics computed during existing batch/stream jobs.

C: Embed a daily sentinel job inside the existing pipeline: compute compact sketches for key features and model scores (quantiles + top-category frequencies) and compare to a rolling 7-day reference window using distance metrics (e.g., PSI/JS on binned histograms). Trigger only if the distance exceeds threshold for 2 consecutive runs (persistence) to avoid noise. Store sketches as small artifacts alongside regular logs and emit a standard alert through the current logging channel. Reference window + sketch choice + persistence policy catches drift within 24 hours without new services.

A: Design a method to reduce bike theft in a city block without adding police patrols. Constraints: $500 budget, cannot install cameras.

B.rule: Treat theft as a risk-reward-time tradeoff; control knobs are time-to-steal (friction), visibility to bystanders (ambient notice), and uncertainty for the thief (randomized deterrents).
B.unlocks: Increase perceived time and uncertainty without surveillance.
B.justification: Most theft is opportunistic; small increases in time and uncertainty push thieves to easier targets.

C: Create ‘high-friction zones’ at racks: add bright motion-activated lights (visibility) and install a loud, non-camera motion chime near the racks that triggers unpredictably (uncertainty). Provide free/cheap secondary locking loops (e.g., community chain) that add an extra step (time-to-steal). Post a simple sign: “Area uses randomized audible deterrents; bikes double-locked.” Time friction + visibility + uncertainty reduces opportunistic theft within budget, without cameras or police.

A: Propose a way to make remote onboarding effective in 5 days for new hires. Constraints: no extra meetings beyond 1 hour/day; must ensure they can ship a small change.

B.rule: Treat onboarding as progressive access + feedback loops; control knobs are task granularity (small shippable steps), scaffolding level (templates/checklists), and feedback latency (time-to-review).
B.unlocks: A 5-day ramp that guarantees a shipped change via tightly-scaffolded micro-deliverables.
B.justification: Competence builds fastest when the learner gets rapid, concrete feedback on real tasks with controlled complexity.

C: Set a 5-day “ship ladder”: Day 1 run-and-test locally (scaffolded checklist), Day 2 edit docs/test (tiny PR), Day 3 change a config/flag (low risk), Day 4 small code change behind a flag, Day 5 enable flag in staging with metrics note. Limit live meetings to 1 hour/day by using async review with an SLA (same-day review). Granularity + scaffolding + fast feedback ensures they ship safely within 5 days.

A: Design a way to reduce mistakes when entering long numbers (invoice IDs) into a legacy system. Constraints: can’t change the system UI; must reduce errors by 60%.

B.rule: Treat entry as a noisy channel; control knobs are redundancy (check digits / chunking), independent verification (second representation), and error visibility (immediate local validation).
B.unlocks: Add error-detection around the UI using human-perceptual redundancy.
B.justification: You can’t change the receiver (UI), but you can change the encoding and verification steps to detect/avoid transmission errors.

C: Introduce a ‘speakable chunk code’ alongside the ID: chunk into groups of 3–4 digits plus a simple check digit (mod 10) printed on invoices. Users enter chunk-by-chunk and verify each chunk by reading it aloud (or pointing) against the printed chunks; at the end they compute/confirm the check digit with a pocket rule card. Add a local pre-entry verification: write the chunks on scratch paper then copy into UI once (two representations). Redundancy + chunking + check digit makes most errors detectable before submission.

A: Propose a method to make a busy playground safer without reducing play freedom. Constraints: no fences, no extra staff, low cost.

B.rule: Treat injuries as high-energy collisions at conflict hotspots; control knobs are flow separation (where running happens), energy dissipation (soft zones), and visibility at intersections (line-of-sight).
B.unlocks: Preserve freedom by reshaping flows and reducing collision energy rather than restricting behavior.
B.justification: Most incidents cluster at crossings and hard landings; changing geometry and surfaces reduces harm while letting kids play.

C: Paint a ‘run loop’ path around the perimeter so high-speed play has a dedicated lane, and mark ‘slow zones’ around climbing/slide exits with bright ground shapes kids naturally avoid running through. Add low-cost soft landing mats only at the top 3 fall-risk points. Improve line-of-sight by relocating one movable object (bench/bin) that blocks a crossing. Flow separation + soft zones + visibility reduces collisions without banning play.

A: Design a way to keep a small community fridge (free food sharing) from being taken over by a few people. Constraints: no ID checks, no surveillance, must stay welcoming.

B.rule: Treat access as a commons allocation problem; control knobs are rationing by friction (small extra step), visibility of norms (what’s fair), and replenishment feedback (signal when to leave).
B.unlocks: Soft constraints that discourage hoarding while preserving dignity and anonymity.
B.justification: Commons fail when marginal taking is easy and norms are unclear; lightweight friction plus clear fairness norms reduces overuse.

C: Use a ‘take-one, leave-time’ norm system: baskets labeled by category with a simple fairness cue (“please take max 2 items per category”). Add a friction step that’s welcoming: each item has a removable “share tag” and you must drop the tag into a clear jar after taking; when the jar fills, it visually signals over-take and prompts self-correction without IDs. Post a replenishment signal: volunteers restock only when tags-per-hour stays below a threshold, encouraging spread-out use. Friction + norm visibility + feedback improves fairness without policing.

A: Propose a way to reduce repeated outages caused by a single flaky microservice dependency. Constraints: can’t rewrite the service; must improve end-to-end availability in 2 weeks.
D: Ask that team to fix it

B.rule: Treat the dependency as an unreliable component in a circuit; control knobs are isolation (bulkheads), degradation mode (serve stale/partial), and backpressure (shed load before collapse).
B.unlocks: Prevent cascade failures by isolating and degrading gracefully when the dependency misbehaves.
B.justification: Outages are often cascades; availability improves fastest by preventing one failure from consuming shared resources.

C: Wrap the dependency calls with a circuit breaker and bulkhead: cap concurrent requests to that service and fail fast when error rate spikes. Provide a defined degraded response (stale cache, default value, or ‘try later’ with retry-after) so upstream services don’t hang. Add backpressure: if breaker is open, shed optional features and skip non-critical calls. Isolation + graceful degradation + load shedding improves end-to-end availability quickly without rewriting the flaky service.

A: Design a way to improve fairness in speaking time during team discussions without a facilitator. Constraints: must feel natural; no timers.

B.rule: Treat discussion as turn-taking with hidden monopolies; control knobs are turn cost (small friction to continue), turn guarantee (easy entry for quiet voices), and topic segmentation (reset turns per topic).
B.unlocks: Self-enforcing turn balance without explicit timing.
B.justification: Dominance persists when continuing is effortless and entering is costly; small continuation friction and topic resets rebalance naturally.

C: Use a ‘two-turn then yield’ norm tied to topic cards: for each topic, anyone may speak twice before they must ask a question to someone else or explicitly yield. Quiet voices get a guaranteed entry via a simple prompt: before switching topics, anyone can add one “+1 or concern” sentence. Topic segmentation resets the turns naturally, and the continuation cost (must ask/yield) reduces monopolies without timers or a facilitator.

A: Propose a way to keep a pet water bowl from getting dirty quickly in a dusty home. Constraints: no new bowls; only rearrangement and simple household items.

B.rule: Treat contamination as particle deposition driven by airflow and splash; control knobs are airflow exposure (where the bowl sits), rim shielding (barrier height), and splash energy (bowl stability).
B.unlocks: Reduce deposition by moving out of particle streams and adding passive shielding.
B.justification: Dust accumulation depends strongly on local airflow and turbulence; shielding and calmer flow reduce particles entering water.

C: Relocate the bowl to a low-airflow corner away from doorways/fans and below waist height (less cross-draft). Create a simple rim shield: cut a ring from a clean plastic container or use a folded towel “donut” around the bowl so airborne dust hits the barrier first. Stabilize the bowl on a non-slip surface (towel/mat) to reduce splash that wets nearby dust and pulls it into the water. Airflow exposure + rim shielding + splash control keeps water cleaner longer without buying a new bowl.

A: Design a way to reduce missed deadlines in a research team where tasks are ambiguous. Constraints: no extra meetings; must work asynchronously.

B.rule: Treat ambiguous tasks as hypothesis-to-artifact pipelines; control knobs are definition of done (artifact), risk checkpoints (early proof), and scope caps (time-boxed slices).
B.unlocks: Convert ambiguity into deliverables with early evidence and bounded slices.
B.justification: Deadlines slip when ‘done’ is fuzzy; requiring small artifacts and early proofs makes progress measurable and predictable.

C: Enforce an async ‘artifact-first’ rule: every task starts with a one-paragraph artifact spec (what file/plot/PR exists when done) and a 48-hour checkpoint artifact (toy result, baseline run, or failure report). Scope is capped by time-box: if not done by the deadline, the deliverable becomes a short write-up plus next experiment plan. Artifact definition + early proof + scope cap reduces ambiguity-driven slippage without adding meetings.

A: Propose a low-cost way to make public trash bins less likely to overflow on weekends. Constraints: pickup schedule can’t change.

B.rule: Treat overflow as capacity + compaction failure under peak load; control knobs are local buffering (extra bag capacity), compaction behavior (make compressing easy), and load distribution (redirect to underused bins).
B.unlocks: Increase effective capacity and spread peak load without changing pickups.
B.justification: Weekend spikes exceed effective capacity; compaction and redistribution can raise effective capacity immediately.

C: Add a weekend ‘buffer liner’: a second bag liner clipped inside the bin that can be deployed when the first fills, doubling usable volume without changing the container. Attach a simple foot-compaction plate (cheap plywood/rubber) that lets users compress trash hygienically, increasing density. Place a bright “bin full? next bin →” arrow sticker that points to a nearby underused bin to distribute load. Buffering + compaction + redirection reduces overflow without pickup changes.

A: Design a method to reduce errors when transferring medication instructions from doctor notes into patient discharge papers. Constraints: no new software; must fit within existing workflow.

B.rule: Treat transcription as a structured mapping problem; control knobs are schema normalization (standard fields), redundancy (independent cross-check), and ambiguity elimination (forced choices over free text).
B.unlocks: Reduce free-text copying and force mismatches to surface before discharge.
B.justification: Errors come from ambiguous, unstructured input; standard schemas and redundant verification catch mismaps early.

C: Introduce a single-page normalized meds schema used during discharge: medication name, dose, timing, duration, and ‘change vs continue’ status—no free-text paragraphs. The transcriber must fill each field from the doctor note and then perform an independent cross-check: a second person verifies only the deltas vs the pre-admission med list (not the whole sheet). Ambiguities trigger a forced-choice escalation (call doctor) rather than guesswork. Normalized fields + delta-focused redundancy + forced-choice handling cuts transcription errors without new software.

A: Propose a way to reduce churn in a subscription product by targeting users at risk, without collecting new personal data. Constraints: interventions must be in-product only.

B.rule: Treat churn as a loss of value realization; control knobs are time-to-first-value (TTFV), habit loop frequency (repeat triggers), and friction removal at the ‘stuck’ step identified from product events (not personal data).
B.unlocks: Event-based rescue flows that restore value realization without profiling new attributes.
B.justification: You can predict churn from behavior signals (stalling, feature non-adoption); fixing the value gap reduces churn more reliably than discounts.

C: Define three at-risk patterns purely from existing product events: (1) stalled before first success, (2) repeated failure at one step, (3) usage drop after a feature change. For each, add an in-product rescue: a one-screen guided action that completes the stuck step, a ‘safe mode’ preset that avoids complexity, or a one-click rollback of the last configuration change. Tune knobs: shorten TTFV by prefilled defaults, increase habit frequency via a lightweight recurring in-product cue (not a notification), and remove friction at the stuck step. Behavioral targeting + value restoration reduces churn without new personal data.

A: Design a way to keep a shared Wi-Fi network stable in a co-working space where heavy users hog bandwidth. Constraints: cannot buy new router; must be fair.

B.rule: Treat Wi-Fi as a shared medium with contention; control knobs are access separation (two SSIDs with different priorities), rate limits (per-device caps), and congestion signaling (make heavy use visible via slow lane).
B.unlocks: Fairness via traffic shaping and class separation without new hardware.
B.justification: Network fairness improves by isolating latency-sensitive traffic and capping hogs; class separation prevents one user from dominating airtime.

C: Create two SSIDs on the existing router: ‘Work’ (priority) and ‘Bulk’ (deprioritized). Apply per-device bandwidth caps on ‘Work’ (enough for calls/docs) and allow higher throughput only on ‘Bulk’ for updates/backups. Make the rule self-enforcing: signage instructs large downloads to use ‘Bulk,’ which will feel slower if misused (congestion signaling). Access separation + per-device caps + slow-lane signaling stabilizes the shared network fairly without new hardware.

A: Propose a way to reduce drop-offs in an online form with 12 fields, while keeping data quality. Constraints: cannot remove fields; must improve completion by 15%.

B.rule: Treat the form as a progressive commitment funnel; control knobs are uncertainty reduction (examples/validation), effort distribution (chunking + save states), and error cost (early detection vs end-of-form).
B.unlocks: Increase completion by lowering perceived risk and preventing late-stage failure.
B.justification: People drop when effort feels front-loaded or errors appear late; progressive commitment and early validation keep momentum without reducing rigor.

C: Chunk the 12 fields into 3 short steps with a visible progress bar and automatic save after each step. Add inline validation and concrete examples for the top 3 confusion fields so errors are caught immediately. Defer only non-dependent fields to later steps so users feel progress early, but keep strict validation on submission. Chunking + early validation + auto-save reduces uncertainty and late failure, raising completion while preserving data quality.

A: Design a way to keep cut flowers fresh for 5 days during a hot week. Constraints: no refrigeration; only a vase, sugar, salt, scissors, and tap water.
D: Put the vase in a cooler room

B.rule: Treat the vase as a competing-process system: hydration via xylem flow versus blockage/microbial growth; control knobs are stem permeability (fresh cut), solute balance (osmotic pressure), and microbial load (water clarity).
B.unlocks: A schedule that keeps flow channels open while maintaining enough osmotic pull to feed petals.
B.justification: Flowers fail when transport clogs or water potential collapses; controlling permeability, osmotic conditions, and microbes preserves hydration.

C: Every morning, recut 1–2 cm of stem under running water to reopen flow channels, then refill with fresh water. Add a tiny pinch of sugar to maintain osmotic pull, and an even tinier pinch of salt to discourage microbial growth (keep both minimal to avoid burn). Remove any leaves below the waterline to reduce contamination. If flowers start drooping, increase permeability by recutting again; if water turns cloudy, reset microbial load by changing water immediately. Permeability + solute balance + microbial control keeps petals hydrated longer without refrigeration.

A: Propose a method to help a child fall asleep faster without screens or medication. Constraints: total routine ≤12 minutes; must work in a noisy home.

B.rule: Treat sleep onset as a state transition driven by arousal gating; control knobs are sensory load (input reduction), predictability (fixed sequence), and parasympathetic activation (slow exhale + muscle release).
B.unlocks: A short, repeatable ‘state machine’ that reliably lowers arousal even under external noise.
B.justification: Fast sleep routines work when they reduce uncertainty and sensory input while triggering a consistent physiological downshift.

C: Run a fixed 12-minute “3-gate” routine: (1) sensory gate: dim lights and give a ‘noise blanket’ (fan or steady hum) to mask sharp sounds, (2) predictability gate: the same 3-sentence story every night (not a long one), (3) physiology gate: 6 slow breaths with long exhale while doing a quick toe-to-face muscle ‘squeeze then melt.’ Keep words minimal and sequence identical. Sensory masking + fixed sequence + long-exhale/muscle release reliably pushes arousal below the sleep threshold even if the house is noisy.

A: Design a fair way to share a single washing machine among 8 roommates. Constraints: no apps, no money transfers, conflicts have been common.

B.rule: Treat the washer as a scarce resource with peak demand; control knobs are reservation tokens (rights to a time slot), expiration (use-it-or-lose-it), and swap rules (how trades happen).
B.unlocks: A low-friction token market that prevents squatting and allows flexibility without arguments.
B.justification: Conflicts persist when claims are ambiguous; explicit tokens with expiration create clarity and enable fair swaps.

C: Give each roommate two physical ‘washer tokens’ per week. A token reserves one 2-hour block by clipping it to a paper time grid on the wall; tokens expire if not started within 15 minutes of the slot (use-it-or-lose-it). Swaps are allowed only by physically exchanging tokens, not verbal promises. Emergency use is one designated daily ‘wildcard’ slot that can be taken only if no one has clipped a token by noon. Tokens + expiration + explicit swap rule make access fair, flexible, and conflict-resistant without apps.

A: Propose a method to reduce food cravings in the afternoon without caffeine or snacks. Constraints: must take ≤3 minutes; usable at work.

B.rule: Treat cravings as an interoception + cue loop: a bodily signal plus a learned trigger; control knobs are mouth sensation (oral substitute), attention re-anchoring (brief competing task), and glucose-stability proxy (micro-movement).
B.unlocks: Interrupt the loop by changing body cues and breaking the learned association quickly.
B.justification: Cravings often fade if you alter sensory input and attention long enough for the cue-driven urge to decay.

C: Do a 3-minute ‘loop interrupt’: (1) mouth cue: rinse mouth with water and hold it 10 seconds (strong oral signal), (2) attention cue: do a 45-second counting task that uses working memory (count backward by 7s or name cities alphabetically), (3) body cue: 60–90 seconds of brisk calf raises or stair steps to shift physiology slightly. Mouth sensation + attention re-anchoring + micro-movement breaks the cue loop so the craving peak passes without snacks or caffeine.

A: Design a way to prevent sand from getting into a beach picnic food container. Constraints: no extra containers; only a cloth, rubber band, and a bottle of water.

B.rule: Treat sand intrusion as particle transport driven by wind and contact; control knobs are seal geometry (gap blocking), surface wetness (particle adhesion), and exposure time (open-close duty cycle).
B.unlocks: Create a temporary particle barrier and reduce the time the opening is vulnerable.
B.justification: Most sand gets in during brief exposures; improving seal geometry and using moisture to trap particles reduces ingress sharply.

C: Wrap the cloth around the container rim so it forms an overhanging ‘skirt’ that blocks the lid gap, then secure it with the rubber band. Lightly wet the outer cloth surface with a few drops of water so airborne sand sticks to the cloth instead of bouncing into the opening. Use a strict duty cycle: open only for a quick serve, then close immediately and shake the cloth skirt away from the opening before reopening. Seal geometry + wet adhesion + reduced exposure time keeps sand out without extra containers.

A: Propose a way to help a team learn a new skill (e.g., presentation) in 4 weeks without adding meeting hours. Constraints: practice must fit into existing work.

B.rule: Treat skill-building as spaced micro-reps with immediate feedback; control knobs are rep size (≤2 minutes), feedback latency (same-day), and difficulty ramp (one variable at a time).
B.unlocks: Skill gains through embedded micro-practice in normal work artifacts.
B.justification: Learning sticks when practice is frequent, small, and corrected quickly; big workshops without reps decay fast.

C: Embed ‘micro-reps’ into existing work: each week, everyone adds one 60-second spoken summary to a doc or demo they already produce. Week 1 focus only on structure (one-sentence claim + 3 bullets), week 2 add clarity (remove filler), week 3 add delivery (pace), week 4 add audience hook. A peer gives same-day feedback using a 3-line template tied to that week’s single variable. Small rep size + fast feedback + one-variable ramp builds skill without extra meeting hours.

A: Design a method to reduce mosquito bites while sleeping without nets or repellents. Constraints: only a fan, a bedsheet, and a light source.

B.rule: Treat bites as a target-acquisition and landing problem; control knobs are airflow at skin (disrupt flight/odor plume), access geometry (reduce exposed skin edges), and attractor placement (light as decoy away from bed).
B.unlocks: Make the bed a low-landing, low-signal zone while diverting mosquitoes elsewhere.
B.justification: Mosquitoes home in on CO₂/odor plumes and need stable air to land; airflow and access barriers reduce successful landings.

C: Place the light source across the room near a wall (decoy zone) and keep the bed area dark. Run the fan so airflow crosses the sleeping surface (not above your head), disrupting the odor plume and making landing difficult. Tuck the bedsheet to minimize exposed edges—leave a single small breathing gap but avoid loose folds where mosquitoes can crawl under. Airflow disruption + access geometry + decoy placement reduces bites without nets or repellents.

A: Propose a low-cost method to reduce mirror fogging in a bathroom with no ventilation fan. Constraints: can’t install hardware; only soap, a towel, and hot/cold water.

B.rule: Treat fogging as condensation nucleation on a cooler surface; control knobs are surface film (surfactant layer), mirror temperature (pre-warm), and vapor load timing (when humidity spikes).
B.unlocks: Reduce droplet formation by changing surface wetting and minimizing temperature gradient.
B.justification: A surfactant film turns droplets into a uniform sheet, and pre-warming reduces the mirror’s condensation driving force.

C: Before showering, rinse the mirror with warm water for 10–15 seconds to pre-warm it, then apply a tiny amount of soap and buff it to a thin invisible film with the towel (surfactant layer). During the shower, keep the door cracked only at the end to dump vapor quickly. If fog starts, splash a little warm water on the mirror rather than wiping (reduces droplet nucleation). Surface film + pre-warm + vapor timing keeps the mirror clear without installing a fan.

A: Design a way to keep a backpack’s shoulder straps from slipping off a smooth jacket. Constraints: no sewing; only a safety pin, a rubber band, and a scarf.

B.rule: Treat slipping as low-friction contact plus lateral drift; control knobs are friction increase (texture), geometry constraint (add a lateral link), and load path (where tension pulls).
B.unlocks: Create a non-slip interface and a drift-stop without permanent modifications.
B.justification: Straps slip when normal force is low and nothing prevents sideways motion; adding texture and a lateral constraint stabilizes them.

C: Place the scarf over your shoulders as a high-texture interface, then put the backpack straps on top of the scarf. Use the rubber band as a small lateral link by looping it between the two straps across your chest (not tight—just enough to prevent drift). If needed, anchor the scarf to the jacket collar area with a safety pin (pin scarf-to-scarf fold, not jacket fabric) so it doesn’t slide. Friction + lateral constraint + stable load path prevents strap slip without sewing.

A: Propose a way to reduce bruising when carrying grocery bags by hand. Constraints: no new bags; only a towel and a belt.

B.rule: Treat pain as pressure concentration on soft tissue; control knobs are contact area (spread load), load sharing (distribute to stronger structures), and leverage (reduce finger torque).
B.unlocks: Convert point loads into distributed loads using improvised padding and a load-sharing loop.
B.justification: Bruising comes from high local pressure; increasing contact area and shifting load to forearm/shoulder reduces peak stress.

C: Thread the belt through bag handles to create a single load-sharing loop, then wrap the towel around the belt section where it contacts your hand/forearm to increase contact area. Carry the loop over your forearm near the elbow (stronger structure) rather than in your fingers; keep the belt taut so bags don’t swing (reduces torque). Contact area + load sharing + reduced leverage on fingers cuts bruising without changing bags.

A: Design a way to make a spicy dish feel milder without changing ingredients. Constraints: you can only change how it’s served and eaten.

B.rule: Treat perceived spice as receptor exposure over time; control knobs are dilution geometry (bite composition), temperature (volatility/sensation), and contact time (how long it sits on tongue).
B.unlocks: Reduce peak exposure by changing bite structure and thermal/contact dynamics.
B.justification: Capsaicin perception scales with concentration at receptors and dwell time; changing bite mix and temperature changes the exposure profile.

C: Serve the dish alongside a large neutral ‘carrier’ (plain rice/bread) and enforce a bite rule: each spicy bite must be paired with 2–3x carrier in the same mouthful (dilution geometry). Let the dish cool slightly before eating (temperature knob), and take smaller bites with quick swallow rather than letting it linger (contact time). Bite mixing + lower temperature + shorter dwell reduces peak receptor exposure without changing the recipe.

A: Propose a method to stop a squeaky door in a rented apartment without oils or tools. Constraints: only paper, soap, and water; must be removable.

B.rule: Treat squeak as stick-slip friction at a contact interface; control knobs are lubrication film (soap), contact pressure distribution (shim thickness), and motion speed (avoid resonance).
B.unlocks: Interrupt stick-slip by adding a removable low-shear film and smoothing contact pressure.
B.justification: Squeak is oscillation from stick-slip; lowering static friction and evening pressure breaks the cycle.

C: Make a thin soap slurry (soap + a few drops of water) and apply a tiny amount to the hinge pin area using a rolled paper ‘wick’ so it coats without dripping. If the door rubs the frame, fold paper into a thin shim and temporarily place it at the rubbing point to redistribute pressure. Then cycle the door 10–15 times at moderate speed to spread the film. Lubrication film + pressure smoothing + controlled motion eliminates stick-slip while staying removable.

A: Design a way to keep a phone stable for filming while walking slowly. Constraints: no gimbal; only a shoelace and a small towel.
D: Hold the phone with two hands

B.rule: Treat shake as a high-frequency vibration transmitted through arms; control knobs are tension (shoelace), damping (towel), and mass distribution (where the phone sits).
B.unlocks: Build a passive stabilizer by creating a tensioned, damped support rather than relying on grip strength.
B.justification: A tensioned line creates a stable reference; damping absorbs micro-jitters, reducing high-frequency motion in the camera.

C: Create a shoelace neck/torso tether: tie the shoelace into a loop around your neck and attach the loop to the phone (or your hands holding it) so there is gentle forward tension when you extend your arms. Wrap the towel around the phone as a damping pad between your hands and the device, and keep the phone centered near your body (mass distribution). Adjust tension so it’s taut but comfortable; higher tension reduces sway, more towel contact increases damping. Tension + damping + centered mass yields smoother walking footage without a gimbal.

A: Propose a way to reduce arguments about chores between partners. Constraints: no apps, no charts on the wall, and it must feel fair.

B.rule: Treat chore conflict as an invisibility + control problem; control knobs are observability (what work is seen), autonomy (who chooses how), and renegotiation frequency (how often rules reset).
B.unlocks: A system where effort becomes visible without policing, and choices feel autonomous.
B.justification: Resentment grows when work is unseen and control feels one-sided; making effort visible and resetting agreements prevents accumulation.

C: Use a weekly 10-minute ‘reset’ conversation with a fixed script: each person names (1) one chore they’ll own this week (autonomy), (2) one chore they want done but don’t want to manage, and (3) one constraint (busy day). During the week, use only a small, private ‘done cue’ that’s natural—not a chart: e.g., when a task is done, place a single shared token (a coaster) on a specific shelf; at week end you clear it together (observability without tracking who did what). Reset weekly so imbalances don’t compound. Observability + autonomy + frequent reset reduces arguments while staying fair.

A: Design a method to reduce eye strain during long reading sessions. Constraints: no special glasses; only lighting adjustments and breaks; must not reduce reading speed.

B.rule: Treat strain as a fatigue loop driven by accommodation, dryness, and contrast; control knobs are focal distance variation (accommodation reset), blink lubrication (dryness), and glare/contrast balance (lighting geometry).
B.unlocks: Keep speed by preventing fatigue buildup rather than slowing reading.
B.justification: Short, structured resets prevent the nonlinear jump in discomfort that later forces longer breaks.

C: Use a 20-second ‘accommodation reset’ every 8–10 minutes: look at the farthest point in the room while doing 5 slow blinks (lubrication) without stopping the reading session for long. Set lighting so the page/screen is brighter than the surroundings but not reflective: move the lamp to the side to avoid glare (geometry). Keep text contrast stable (avoid bright reflections) so eyes don’t constantly re-adapt. Distance variation + blink lubrication + glare control reduces strain without slowing reading pace.

A: Propose a way to keep a candle from dripping wax onto a table. Constraints: no candle holder; only aluminum foil and a glass.
D: Let it drip and clean later

B.rule: Treat dripping as a melt-flow management problem; control knobs are heat distribution (wick length + draft), catchment geometry (foil shape), and thermal sink (glass mass).
B.unlocks: Stabilize melt pool and route wax into a controlled catchment.
B.justification: Wax drips when melt pool becomes asymmetric or overheated; shaping airflow/heat and providing a catch basin prevents spills.

C: Place the candle in the glass so the glass acts as a thermal sink and wind shield, reducing asymmetry. Wrap aluminum foil around the candle base to form a wide ‘gutter’ lip that sits over the glass rim or around the base, directing any wax inward. Keep the wick short (heat distribution) by pinching off the charred tip once cooled. Heat stabilization + catchment geometry + glass thermal buffering prevents wax from reaching the table.

A: Design a way to keep a pot from boiling over on a stove without lowering the heat too much. Constraints: you can use a wooden spoon and adjust lid position.

B.rule: Treat boil-over as foam stability plus nucleation; control knobs are bubble breakup (mechanical disruption), headspace (lid gap), and surface wetting (interrupt foam film).
B.unlocks: Prevent stable foam from forming a continuous plug that climbs out of the pot.
B.justification: Boil-over happens when foam becomes stable and fills headspace; breaking foam and increasing venting stops runaway overflow.

C: Lay a wooden spoon across the top so rising foam contacts it and breaks (bubble breakup). Keep the lid slightly ajar to increase venting and headspace, preventing pressure/steam from stabilizing foam. If foam starts climbing, briefly lift and replace the spoon to reset the surface film. Bubble breakup + headspace/venting + foam-film interruption prevents boil-over while maintaining high heat.

A: Propose a method to keep bread crust crisp for 3 hours after baking. Constraints: no oven reheat; only a paper bag and a towel.
D: Put it in a plastic bag

B.rule: Treat crispness as a moisture diffusion balance: crust loses crispness when vapor is trapped; control knobs are permeability (paper vs towel), thermal gradient (cooling rate), and contact area (where steam condenses).
B.unlocks: Vent moisture while avoiding excessive drying of the interior.
B.justification: A crisp crust needs moisture to escape; breathable layers manage humidity better than sealed containers.

C: Let the bread cool on a rack (or propped) for 20–30 minutes so major steam leaves, then place it in a paper bag (permeability) with the towel wrapped loosely around the bag—not around the bread—to reduce rapid cooling without trapping vapor. Keep the loaf positioned so minimal surface touches the bag (reduce condensation contact). Permeability + controlled cooling + low contact area keeps crust crisp for hours without reheat.

A: Design a way to keep a small boat (kayak) from drifting while you stop for 10 minutes near shore. Constraints: no anchor; only a rope and a heavy rock.
D: Use the paddle as an anchor

B.rule: Treat drifting as force balance under waves/wind; control knobs are scope (rope length), attachment geometry (angle to shore), and friction/holding (rock placement).
B.unlocks: Create a stable mooring by maximizing holding friction and reducing lateral pull.
B.justification: Mooring stability depends on line angle and scope; longer scope lowers peak tension and reduces drag-induced jerks.

C: Place the rock well above the waterline where it can’t roll easily, then run the rope from the kayak bow to the rock with long scope so the line angle is shallow (reduces tension spikes). Position the kayak so wind/wave forces pull mostly along the rope axis rather than sideways by choosing a point slightly upwind of where you stop (attachment geometry). If needed, wedge the rock into a depression to increase friction. Scope + geometry + rock friction keeps the kayak from drifting for a short stop without an anchor.

A: Propose a method to keep a salad from getting soggy for 6 hours. Constraints: no fridge; only a container, paper towel, and dressing.

B.rule: Treat sogginess as liquid migration into porous leaves; control knobs are phase separation (keep liquids away), humidity buffering (paper towel), and contact time (when mixing happens).
B.unlocks: Delay wetting while managing condensation inside the container.
B.justification: Texture fails when leaves stay in contact with free liquid; separating phases and buffering moisture preserves crispness.

C: Line the container lid (not the base) with a dry paper towel to absorb condensation from above, and keep dressing in a small sealed corner (or wrapped) so it doesn’t contact leaves. Pack wet ingredients (tomatoes/cucumbers) at the bottom separated by another small paper towel layer, with greens on top. Mix only at eating time. Phase separation + humidity buffering + delayed contact preserves crispness for hours without refrigeration.

A: Design a method to keep a sleeping baby’s room dark during daytime naps. Constraints: no curtains, no tape on walls, only a bedsheet, clothespins, and a lamp.
D: Move the crib away from the window
Hang a blanket over the window
Ask the landlord to install curtains
Let the baby get used to light

B.rule: Treat light control as a geometry + reflection problem: photons enter along specific angles; control knobs are occlusion coverage (where you block), edge sealing (light leaks), and contrast management (reduce bright reflections inside).
B.unlocks: A removable ‘light baffle’ that blocks direct rays and suppresses leaks without adhesives.
B.justification: Most daytime brightness comes from direct beams and edge leaks; blocking lines-of-sight and sealing edges drops illuminance dramatically.

C: Make a sheet ‘baffle canopy’ by draping the bedsheet over the window area and clipping it to furniture (chair backs) with clothespins so it sits several centimeters away from the glass (reduces heat and allows airflow).
