Create edge sealing by folding the sheet edges into thick hems and clipping those hems tight at the sides to block light leaks. Finally, aim the lamp at the sheet (not the room) on very low to create a uniform dim background, reducing contrast between window area and room. Occlusion + edge sealing + contrast control produces nap-darkness without curtains or tape.

A: Propose a way to stop a bicycle chain from squealing during a ride when you have no lubricant. Constraints: you can stop once; you have water, a small brush, and a cloth.

B.rule: Treat squeal as boundary contamination + stick-slip at link interfaces; control knobs are contaminant removal (grit), surface drying (avoid water film noise), and load distribution (gear choice/torque).
B.unlocks: Quieting by cleaning and minimizing stick-slip forces, not by lubrication.
B.justification: Grit increases friction and produces stick-slip; removing it and reducing torque spikes often eliminates noise temporarily.

C: Stop once and thoroughly rinse the chain while back-pedaling to flush grit, then use the brush to dislodge packed dirt at the jockey wheels and chainrings. Wipe aggressively with the cloth until the chain feels dry to the touch (water films can also squeak). For the rest of the ride, stay in an easier gear and spin faster to reduce peak torque on each link. Grit removal + drying + lower torque reduces stick-slip enough to stop squealing until you can properly lube later.

A: Design a way to keep a suitcase from tipping over when packed unevenly. Constraints: no repacking; only a belt and two small books.

B.rule: Treat tipping as a center-of-mass outside the support polygon; control knobs are base width (add outriggers), constraint geometry (belt as tie-down), and contact friction (books as wedges).
B.unlocks: Stability by enlarging the support base and adding a restoring constraint, without changing internal mass.
B.justification: An object tips when its COM crosses its base; widening the base and adding a stabilizing constraint increases tipping threshold.

C: Place the two books on the tipping side as external ‘outriggers’ under the suitcase edge to widen the base and shift the effective support polygon. Use the belt to strap the books to the suitcase so they move as a unit, and tighten the belt to pull the suitcase slightly toward the stable side (restoring constraint). If the floor is slippery, angle the books as shallow wedges to increase friction. Base widening + belt constraint + friction wedges prevents tipping without repacking.

A: Propose a method to calm a barking dog when someone knocks, without yelling or treats. Constraints: must work within 2 minutes and be repeatable.
D: Move the dog to another room

B.rule: Treat barking as a trigger → arousal → feedback loop; control knobs are competing behavior (incompatible action), distance from trigger (reduce stimulus intensity), and predictability (same cue sequence).
B.unlocks: Interrupt the loop by swapping the response pattern, not by suppressing emotion directly.
B.justification: Arousal sustains when the dog rehearses barking; an incompatible routine plus reduced stimulus breaks reinforcement quickly.

C: Use a consistent 3-step routine: (1) create distance by calmly stepping between dog and door and backing up 2–3 meters (reduces trigger intensity), (2) cue an incompatible behavior like ‘sit + nose-to-hand’ with your hand held low (keeps mouth occupied and attention anchored), (3) repeat the same quiet breathing posture for 30 seconds. Do this every knock so predictability forms. Competing behavior + reduced intensity + fixed sequence interrupts barking within ~2 minutes without yelling or treats.

A: Design a way to keep a phone from overheating in direct sun at the beach while still using GPS for 1 hour. Constraints: no external power, no ice, only a towel and a water bottle.

B.rule: Treat overheating as an energy balance: absorbed solar radiation vs heat rejection; control knobs are radiative exposure (shade), thermal coupling to a heat sink (water bottle), and duty cycle (GPS sampling intervals).
B.unlocks: Reduce input power and increase passive heat dumping without wetting electronics.
B.justification: Phones overheat mainly from solar load + internal power; shading and coupling to a cooler mass lowers temperature without risky water contact.

C: Wrap the towel to form a small sunshade ‘hood’ over the phone while leaving vents/exposed edges for airflow (reduce radiation input). Place the phone against the side of a cool water bottle through a dry towel layer (thermal coupling without moisture), using the bottle as a heat sink. Switch to a duty cycle: check GPS intermittently (e.g., wake every 1–2 minutes) instead of keeping the screen on continuously. Shade + heat-sink coupling + reduced duty cycle maintains GPS use for an hour without overheating.

A: Propose a way to keep soup hot for 2 hours during a picnic. Constraints: no thermos; only a pot with lid, newspaper, and a backpack.
D: Wrap the pot in a towel

B.rule: Treat heat loss as conduction + convection + radiation; control knobs are insulation thickness (newspaper layers), trapped air (loft), and surface area exposure (pack geometry).
B.unlocks: Build a high-loft insulating shell around the pot and reduce airflow stripping.
B.justification: Most cooling comes from convective airflow and conduction; layered paper traps air, reducing both when packed snugly.

C: Boil the soup briefly before leaving, fill the pot as full as practical (reduces headspace convection), and keep the lid sealed. Wrap the pot in many layers of newspaper crumpled slightly to maximize trapped air, then place it deep in the backpack surrounded by soft items to reduce airflow. Keep the backpack closed and out of wind. Insulation thickness + trapped-air loft + minimized exposure keeps soup hot for ~2 hours without a thermos.

A: Design a way to reduce bitterness in black coffee without adding sugar or milk. Constraints: only water and temperature control; no extra ingredients.

B.rule: Treat bitterness as extraction and perception: over-extracted compounds dominate at higher temperature; control knobs are dilution ratio (water added), serving temperature (cooling), and sip geometry (mix with water on tongue).
B.unlocks: Lower perceived bitterness by shifting concentration and thermal perception without adding flavor agents.
B.justification: Bitterness intensity scales with concentration and temperature; dilution and cooling reduce receptor activation and harshness.

C: Let the coffee cool 3–5 minutes to lower bitterness perception, then add a small amount of hot water in steps (e.g., 10–20% at a time) until harshness drops while flavor remains. When sipping, alternate a small sip of water with coffee so the tongue experiences partial dilution (sip geometry). Dilution + lower temperature + controlled sipping reduces bitterness without sweeteners or milk.

A: Propose a low-cost method to prevent books from warping in a humid room. Constraints: no dehumidifier; only a box, paper, and a small bag of rice.

B.rule: Treat warping as moisture gradient through pages and covers; control knobs are humidity buffering (rice), airflow exposure (box venting), and mechanical constraint (stack pressure).
B.unlocks: Create a microclimate with lower RH and even moisture to prevent gradients that cause curl.
B.justification: Paper warps when one side gains moisture faster; buffering humidity and constraining shape prevents differential expansion.

C: Make a book microclimate: place books upright and tightly packed in a box with a small vent gap (not airtight). Put rice in an open paper pouch inside the box as a humidity buffer, and add dry paper sheets between covers of the most valuable books to equalize moisture uptake. Keep the stack snug so covers are mechanically constrained. Buffering + controlled venting + mechanical constraint reduces moisture gradients and prevents warping without a dehumidifier.

A: Design a method to keep a classroom quiet during group work without reducing collaboration. Constraints: no punishment; only seating arrangement and one simple classroom signal.

B.rule: Treat noise as positive feedback in a shared channel; control knobs are distance between talkers (acoustic coupling), task coupling (how much groups need to shout), and a real-time gain control signal (visible volume target).
B.unlocks: Reduce channel coupling and add a nonverbal ‘automatic gain control’ cue that groups self-regulate to.
B.justification: Classrooms get loud because groups raise volume to overcome others; lowering coupling and giving a shared target breaks the escalation loop.

C: Arrange tables into ‘acoustic islands’ with maximum spacing and backs not facing each other, so voices don’t directly project into another group (distance/coupling). Give each group a task structure that reduces cross-table shouting (write-first, then share locally). Use one simple signal: a visible hand sign or small meter on the board indicating the current ‘voice level’ target; when it goes down, groups must switch to whisper + write mode. Coupling reduction + task coupling + gain-control signal prevents escalation while preserving collaboration.

A: Propose a way to keep an avocado from browning after cutting. Constraints: no lemon/lime; only salt, water, and a container.
D: Just scrape off brown layer later

B.rule: Treat browning as oxygen-driven enzymatic oxidation; control knobs are oxygen access (water barrier), surface chemistry (salt), and exposed surface area (cut geometry).
B.unlocks: Minimize oxygen contact by using water as an oxygen barrier and salting to slow surface reactions.
B.justification: Oxidation requires oxygen at the surface; reducing oxygen diffusion and altering surface conditions delays browning.

C: Keep the avocado piece as large as possible (minimize surface area), lightly salt the cut face, then place it cut-side-down in a shallow layer of water inside a container so the cut surface is fully submerged (oxygen barrier). Close the container to reduce air exchange. Oxygen blocking + surface chemistry + minimized area keeps it green much longer without citrus.

A: Design a way to reduce slipping on a wet tiled floor in a shop entrance during rain. Constraints: no mats can be purchased; only cardboard boxes and tape.

B.rule: Treat slipping as low-friction contact under a water film; control knobs are surface roughness (texture), water removal path (wicking channels), and coverage area (where traction is needed).
B.unlocks: Create a temporary high-roughness, water-wicking surface from layered cardboard geometry.
B.justification: Traction improves when you break the water film and add asperities; cardboard can wick and create edges that disrupt lubrication.

C: Flatten cardboard into overlapping tiles and score them with many shallow cuts to create raised ridges (roughness). Arrange them so grooves lead outward, encouraging water to wick and drain away from the walking path (removal path). Tape only the perimeter and seams to prevent curling, focusing coverage on the first 2–3 steps where slips happen most. Roughness + wicking channels + targeted coverage reduces slipping using only boxes and tape.

A: Propose a method to keep a child’s backpack organized so homework doesn’t get crushed. Constraints: no new organizers; only two folders and a piece of string.
D: Put papers in the front pocket

B.rule: Treat the backpack as a moving load with compression; control knobs are load partition (hard vs soft zones), stiffness spine (folders as structure), and restraint (string to prevent shifting).
B.unlocks: Create a structural ‘spine’ and a restrained paper zone that resists crushing.
B.justification: Crushing happens when papers float among heavy items; adding stiffness and partitioning keeps loads from collapsing onto homework.

C: Use the two folders as a rigid spine: place them back-to-back against the backpack’s back panel, forming a stiff wall. Put all homework inside the inner folder and position it in the protected spine zone. Use the string to tie a simple loop around the folder spine and the backpack’s internal loop/handle so the spine can’t slide downward. Partition + stiffness + restraint keeps papers flat even when the bag is tossed.

A: Design a way to stop a table from wobbling on an uneven floor. Constraints: no tools; only a napkin and a coin; must be removable.

B.rule: Treat wobble as a missing support point in a 3-point contact system; control knobs are shim thickness (napkin folds), shim placement (which leg), and friction (coin as rigid base).
B.unlocks: Create a stable fourth contact by matching shim thickness to the gap and preventing slip.
B.justification: A wobbly table effectively rests on 3 legs; adding a precisely sized shim to the high-gap leg restores stable support.

C: Identify the ‘floating’ leg by lightly pressing corners. Fold the napkin repeatedly to create a shim of adjustable thickness, then place the coin under the napkin as a rigid, non-compressing base. Slide the napkin+coin under the floating leg until wobble disappears; add or remove a fold to tune thickness. Thickness tuning + correct leg placement + rigid friction base stabilizes the table and stays removable.

A: Propose a method to get a stubborn jar open when you have low grip strength. Constraints: no tools; only a rubber glove and a towel.

B.rule: Treat opening as torque transmission limited by friction and leverage; control knobs are friction coefficient (glove), normal force distribution (towel wrap), and torque arm (two-handed opposing forces).
B.unlocks: Increase effective friction and apply torque with body mechanics rather than finger strength.
B.justification: Most failures are slip, not strength; raising friction and distributing force lets the same muscles generate higher torque.

C: Put the rubber glove on the hand turning the lid to maximize friction. Wrap the towel around the jar body to increase grip and spread normal force over your palm and forearm. Use a counter-torque posture: hold the jar against your torso with the towel hand while turning the lid with the gloved hand, so larger muscle groups contribute. Friction boost + force distribution + better torque arm opens jars without needing more grip strength.

A: Design a way to reduce motion sickness when reading in a moving car. Constraints: no medication; only seat choice and behavior changes.

B.rule: Treat motion sickness as sensory conflict between vestibular input and visual reference; control knobs are visual frame stability (horizon view), head motion (minimize), and exposure duty cycle (read vs reset).
B.unlocks: Reduce conflict by stabilizing the visual frame and limiting conflict duration.
B.justification: Symptoms rise when mismatch persists; giving the brain a stable external frame and frequent resets reduces nausea.

C: Choose the front passenger seat (or middle back) where you can see the road horizon easily. Hold reading material high enough that peripheral vision still captures forward motion cues, and keep your head supported to minimize bobbing. Use a duty cycle: 2 minutes reading, then 20 seconds eyes on horizon to reset sensory alignment. Visual frame stability + minimized head motion + reset duty cycle reduces conflict and motion sickness without medication.

A: Propose a method to stop rice from sticking to the pot after cooking. Constraints: no non-stick pot; only water control and resting time.

B.rule: Treat sticking as starch gel adhesion during final drying; control knobs are water ratio (gel formation), steam redistribution (rest time), and heat tail (how quickly you remove heat).
B.unlocks: Prevent the bottom layer from over-drying into a glue film.
B.justification: Starch becomes adhesive when concentrated and overheated; controlling the end-of-cook moisture and rest equalizes hydration and reduces bonding.

C: Use a slightly higher water ratio than your current baseline (small increments) so the bottom layer never fully dries while heat is on. When water is absorbed, turn off heat and leave the lid closed for 10–12 minutes so steam rehydrates the bottom layer and sets the grains evenly (redistribution). Avoid a long low-heat tail that overcooks the bottom. Water ratio + closed-lid rest + fast heat cutoff reduces starch glue and sticking.

A: Design a way to keep a paper poster flat on a wall for a day without tape or pins. Constraints: only string and a few coins; wall must not be damaged.

B.rule: Treat curling as bending moment from paper memory; control knobs are edge tension (string), distributed weight (coins), and contact pattern (where you apply downward force).
B.unlocks: Flatten by applying gentle distributed tension and edge weighting rather than point fixation.
B.justification: Paper curls because edges lift; adding distributed downward force and edge tension counteracts curl without damaging the surface.

C: Make a ‘weighted hem’ along the bottom edge: fold a small sleeve of paper and slip coins in evenly spaced so weight is distributed. Use string to create a light top tension: tie the string around the poster’s top corners (like a hanger) and hang it from an existing hook/door top/ledge (not the wall). The bottom weighted hem pulls down while the top string keeps corners spread. Edge tension + distributed weight + controlled contact pattern keeps the poster flat for a day without tape or pins.

A: Propose a method to keep ice cream from melting too fast at an outdoor table. Constraints: no cooler; only a bowl, a larger bowl, salt, and water.

B.rule: Treat melting as heat flow into a phase-change system; control knobs are boundary temperature (salt-ice bath equivalent via cold brine), contact area (nested bowl interface), and insulation of top surface (air exposure).
B.unlocks: Create a cold sink around the serving bowl using endothermic brine cooling and reduced heat contact.
B.justification: Lowering the bowl’s surrounding temperature reduces heat influx; salt water can form a colder brine than plain water, slowing melt.

C: Fill the larger bowl with very cold water and add salt to create a chilled brine. Nest the smaller ice cream bowl inside so its sides contact the cold brine (maximize interface), while keeping brine from splashing into the ice cream. Keep the top surface area small by serving in taller scoops and returning the container to the brine between servings. Boundary temperature reduction + high contact area to cold sink + reduced air exposure slows melting without a cooler.

A: Design a method to remember a short list (5 items) for 30 minutes with high accuracy without writing it down. Constraints: must be subtle in public.

B.rule: Treat recall as cue-based retrieval; control knobs are cue distinctiveness (unique imagery), cue order (a fixed route), and rehearsal spacing (brief refreshes).
B.unlocks: A discreet memory palace micro-route that anchors each item to a stable cue with minimal rehearsal.
B.justification: Memory improves when items bind to distinct cues in a known sequence; spaced micro-rehearsal prevents decay without visible behavior.

C: Use a 5-stop micro-route you already know (front door → elevator → street corner → café → checkout). Convert each item into a vivid, odd image and place it at one stop. Do two 10-second silent walk-through rehearsals: immediately after encoding and again 10 minutes later. Distinct imagery + fixed route order + spaced refresh yields reliable 30-minute recall without writing or looking conspicuous.

A: Propose a way to keep a toddler from running off in a crowded park without using a leash or yelling. Constraints: must feel playful and safe.

B.rule: Treat running off as exploration exceeding a safe radius; control knobs are attractive target placement (where the fun is), boundary salience (easy-to-see limits), and intermittent check-ins (game-based returns).
B.unlocks: Keep the child inside a ‘fun gravity well’ with playful boundary cues rather than control force.
B.justification: Toddlers follow attention and rewards; making the safe zone more attractive and boundaries salient reduces escape attempts.

C: Create a ‘home base game’: pick a visible landmark as base and make it the source of fun (bubbles, a short song, a spinning game) every 2–3 minutes. Define a simple boundary like “stay where you can see the red bench,” and reinforce by praising every time they point to the landmark (boundary salience). If they move too far, turn it into a return mission (“bring me a leaf to base”) instead of chasing. Attraction + salient boundary + playful check-ins keeps them close without yelling or leashes.

A: Design a way to reduce email overload for a team of 20 without using new tools. Constraints: must not miss critical messages; no extra meetings.
D: Tell people to send fewer emails

B.rule: Treat email as an interrupt-driven queue with priority inversion; the control knobs are admission control (what qualifies as email), priority labels (critical vs FYI), and aggregation windows (batching non-urgent traffic).
B.unlocks: A low-friction routing policy that preserves critical messages while batching everything else into predictable windows.
B.justification: Overload happens when all messages share the same channel and urgency; separating and batching reduces interrupts without losing signal.

C: Define two subject prefixes: [ACTION-24H] and [FYI-DIGEST]. Only [ACTION-24H] can request a response; everything else must be [FYI-DIGEST] and is sent only in two daily digest windows. Add a rule: if it’s not actionable within 24h, it goes to the digest. This enforces admission control + priority labeling + batching while keeping critical mail visible.

A: Propose a way to improve code review quality without slowing velocity. Constraints: no new tools; must fit existing PR flow.
D: Ask reviewers to be more careful

B.rule: Treat a PR as a set of risk-bearing deltas; tokens of attention should be allocated to the highest-risk surfaces (interfaces, invariants, failure modes) rather than lines changed.
B.unlocks: A risk-first review protocol that increases correctness per minute, keeping throughput stable.
B.justification: Velocity drops when review effort scales with size; focusing on risk concentrates scrutiny where defects are most costly.

C: Require the author to label the PR with exactly 3 risks (API/behavior, data correctness, reliability) and 1 invariant that must hold. Reviewers must comment on each risk/invariant or explicitly ‘pass.’ Everything else is optional. This keeps time bounded while ensuring the review targets the failure surfaces that matter.

A: Design a low-cost method to keep drinking water cool for 6 hours outdoors. Constraints: no ice; only a bottle, a towel, and sunlight/shade.
D: Wrap the bottle in a towel

B.rule: Treat cooling as an evaporative heat exchanger with a boundary-condition control: towel wetness sets evaporation, airflow sets transfer, and sunlight sets radiative load.
B.unlocks: Passive cooling by maximizing evaporation while minimizing solar input.
B.justification: Evaporation removes heat efficiently; blocking radiation prevents the sun from overwhelming the cooling effect.

C: Soak the towel, wring until damp (not dripping), wrap the bottle, and keep it in shade with airflow (hang it, don’t bury it). Re-wet the towel when it dries. Shade cuts radiative heating; evaporation through the towel continuously removes heat from the bottle.

A: Propose a way to reduce misinformation spread in a group chat without moderating content or adding fact-checkers.

B.rule: Treat forwarding as low-friction replication; the control knobs are replication cost (small friction), provenance visibility (where it came from), and branching factor (how many forwards per message).
B.unlocks: Reduce viral spread by adding tiny forwarding friction and making provenance salient, without judging truth.
B.justification: Misinformation wins on speed/replication; slowing replication and exposing provenance reduces spread regardless of content.

C: Introduce a norm: any forwarded claim must include a one-line provenance tag (source + date) and can be forwarded only once per person (‘one-hop rule’). If you can’t add provenance, you must paraphrase as a question. This adds replication cost and reduces branching factor without any content moderation.

A: Design a method to keep a laptop trackpad usable when your hands are sweaty. Constraints: no special products; only paper and a cloth.

B.rule: Treat tracking as a friction/skin-contact problem; control knobs are moisture removal (blot), contact material (paper as low-adhesion interface), and motion mode (reduce continuous drag).
B.unlocks: Stabilize input by controlling the skin–surface boundary layer.
B.justification: Sweat changes friction unpredictably; inserting a consistent interface and removing moisture restores predictable glide.

C: Blot fingertips with the cloth (don’t rub), then rest a small paper strip on the trackpad and slide your finger over the paper to move the cursor (paper becomes the interface). Use tap-to-click and short motions to reduce long drags. Replace paper when damp.

A: Propose a way to reduce late project surprises in a multi-team ML launch. Constraints: no extra meetings; must be async.

B.rule: Treat surprises as hidden dependencies with unobserved state; control knobs are dependency declaration, staleness detection, and early ‘interface contracts’ that fail fast.
B.unlocks: Async visibility via contracts and freshness checks rather than status meetings.
B.justification: Surprises appear when dependency state changes silently; explicit contracts + staleness alarms surface risk early.

C: Create a single shared “dependency manifest” file: each team lists dependencies as contracts (input/output, SLA, owner, last-validated date). A bot-free rule: any PR touching an interface must update the manifest and bump last-validated. Anything stale >7 days is treated as “at risk” and blocks merge until revalidated. Contracts + staleness gating surfaces surprises early without meetings.

A: Design a way to keep a room mosquito-free for 2 hours without repellents or nets. Constraints: only a fan, a bowl of water, and a lamp.

B.rule: Treat mosquitoes as plume-following flyers; control knobs are airflow disruption near humans, alternative attractor placement (light/heat/humidity), and landing surface traps (water surface).
B.unlocks: Make the human zone hard to approach while creating a decoy zone elsewhere.
B.justification: They need stable air to track and land; crossflow blocks landing and decoy cues divert search away.

C: Run the fan to create crossflow across the bed/sofa. Place the lamp across the room above/near the bowl of water to create a decoy zone; keep your area darker. The airflow makes you hard to land on; the decoy concentrates insects away from you.

A: Propose a fair method to choose which tech debt to fix each quarter. Constraints: limited capacity; multiple teams disagree.

B.rule: Treat tech debt as a portfolio of risk-bearing liabilities; control knobs are expected loss (probability × impact), interest rate (drag on velocity), and hedge value (how many future projects it enables).
B.unlocks: A scoring mechanism that makes tradeoffs explicit and comparable across teams.
B.justification: Disagreement persists when value is qualitative; portfolio framing turns it into measurable risk/return.

C: Each item gets three numbers: expected outage loss, velocity interest (hours/week saved), and hedge value (count of dependent initiatives unblocked). Fund the top items under a fixed “debt budget,” but reserve 20% for cross-team hedges with the largest dependency fan-out. This keeps fairness by using a shared scoring model and explicit budget split.

A: Design a low-cost way to keep a small room quieter at night in a shared apartment. Constraints: no construction; only a towel, a door, and movable furniture.

B.rule: Treat noise as leakage through dominant gaps and flanking paths; control knobs are gap sealing (door bottom/sides), soft absorption near the leak, and line-of-sight blocking.
B.unlocks: Target the highest-coupling path instead of treating the whole room.
B.justification: A few gaps dominate transmission; sealing and absorption at the leak yields outsized improvement.

C: Roll the towel into a tight “snake” and seal the door bottom gap; hang the remainder (or a folded section) over the door edge on the hallway side to absorb reflections. Place a tall piece of furniture (wardrobe/bookshelf) inside the room near the door to block line-of-sight sound and add mass. This attacks gap + absorption + blocking where the coupling is strongest.

A: Propose a way to reduce food delivery lateness spikes during dinner rush. Constraints: no hiring; must use existing drivers.

B.rule: Treat the system as a queue with two coupled stages: kitchen prep and driver pickup; control knobs are batching (group pickups), slack buffers (staging), and demand shaping (promises) to avoid saturation.
B.unlocks: Reduce lateness by smoothing utilization near capacity without changing headcount.
B.justification: Delay explodes near saturation; small reductions in peak coupling produce large lateness reductions.

C: Introduce “staged pickup windows”: drivers are assigned to micro-batches of nearby orders with a shared pickup window, and restaurants stage bags in a labeled rack 5 minutes before that window. The app promises slightly later ETAs in the peak 20 minutes to avoid oversubscription. Batching + staging buffer + demand shaping reduces spike lateness without more drivers.

A: Design a method to teach debugging to beginners without lectures. Constraints: 30 minutes; no computers; only paper.

B.rule: Treat debugging as hypothesis testing under partial observability; control knobs are instrumentation (what you check), bisection (where you check), and invariant tracking (what must remain true).
B.unlocks: A hands-on ‘debugging game’ that trains the core scientific method of finding faults.
B.justification: People learn debugging when they practice narrowing causes using checks and invariants, not by hearing tips.

C: Run a paper “mystery program” with numbered steps and hidden fault cards. Students can spend limited ‘probe tokens’ to inspect intermediate values, and must use bisection to locate the earliest step violating an invariant. They win by producing a minimal fix and a test that would catch it next time.

A: Propose a method to keep a shared fridge organized so food doesn't get forgotten. Constraints: no apps; minimal effort daily.
D: Write a list on the door

B.rule: Treat the fridge as a time-ordered cache; control knobs are eviction policy (what must be consumed next), visibility (front-facing), and a small “quarantine” zone for aging items.
B.unlocks: Cache discipline that forces stale items to surface without daily bookkeeping.
B.justification: Waste happens when items become invisible; cache-like rules keep the ‘oldest’ items on the retrieval path.

C: Create two zones: FRONT = “eat first” and BACK = “fresh.” Anything entering the fridge must be placed in BACK; once per day, only the FRONT zone is allowed to be consumed from until it’s empty (then promote from BACK). A small quarantine corner holds items with uncertain dates. This enforces eviction/visibility with near-zero daily overhead.

A: Design a way to keep hands warm while typing in a cold room. Constraints: no heater; only a mug, hot water, and a towel.

B.rule: Treat hand warmth as local blood-flow and contact heat transfer; control knobs are periodic heat charging (mug), insulation (towel), and timing (short recharge cycles).
B.unlocks: A safe thermal buffer that maintains hand temperature without continuous heat.
B.justification: Small periodic heat inputs can maintain comfort if insulated and timed before hands cool too far.

C: Fill the mug with hot water and wrap it in the towel to make a warm “charging station.” Every 5–8 minutes, rest palms around the mug for 10–15 seconds, then return to typing. The towel prevents burns and slows cooling, turning the mug into a safe, reusable heat buffer.

A: Propose a way to make a dataset labeling project more consistent across annotators. Constraints: no extra training time; must start tomorrow.

B.rule: Treat labeling as a decision boundary problem; control knobs are boundary exemplars (hard negatives/positives), adjudication of edge cases, and calibration drift checks (daily small audits).
B.unlocks: Fast alignment by anchoring boundaries with canonical edge-case cards and lightweight drift detection.
B.justification: Inconsistency is mostly at the boundary; anchoring the boundary and checking drift fixes it faster than broad training.

C: Create a 30-item “boundary deck” of edge cases with gold labels and one-sentence rationales. Each day, each annotator labels 5 random boundary cards first; if they miss >1, they must re-read only the relevant rationale before labeling real data. This keeps consistency by boundary anchoring + daily drift checks without extra training sessions.

A: Design a way to reduce glare on a phone screen outdoors. Constraints: no screen protector; only paper and your hands.

B.rule: Treat glare as specular reflection entering your eyes; control knobs are shading geometry (block incident rays), viewing angle, and background contrast.
B.unlocks: Create a temporary light baffle that blocks reflection paths.
B.justification: If you block the main incident light directions, you reduce specular reflection and improve contrast without changing the screen.

C: Fold the paper into a three-sided hood (like a mini visor) and hold it so it shades the screen from the sun while leaving the bottom open for airflow and touch. Adjust your viewing angle so the brightest reflection is directed away from your eyes. The paper hood blocks incident rays, reducing glare immediately.

A: Propose a method to reduce late-night phone scrolling without deleting apps. Constraints: must feel gentle, not restrictive.

B.rule: Treat scrolling as a frictionless reward loop; control knobs are access friction (tiny extra step), cue disruption (change the trigger), and a replacement micro-reward.
B.unlocks: Break the loop by adding minimal friction and swapping the trigger-response chain.
B.justification: Behavior changes when the cost of the habit rises slightly and an alternative reward is available at the same cue.

C: Move your charger to another room and keep the phone on low battery near bedtime (adds access friction). Before bed, place the phone face-down under a book so picking it up requires a deliberate move. Replace the cue with a 60-second ritual (one page of a book or 10 slow breaths) that still provides a small reward. Small friction + cue disruption + substitute reward reduces scrolling without bans.

A: Design a method to stop a backpack zipper from slowly opening while walking. Constraints: no new hardware; only a paperclip and string.
D: Tie a knot in the zipper

B.rule: Treat zipper creep as slider backdrive under vibration; control knobs are a secondary latch (paperclip), pre-tensioned constraint (string), and motion stop geometry.
B.unlocks: Add a mechanical detent that prevents the slider from migrating.
B.justification: Zippers open when the slider can move freely; a small latch turns it into a constrained, non-backdrivable state.

C: Clip the paperclip through the zipper pull and a nearby fabric loop/seam to act as a latch. If no loop exists, tie string to an internal seam and route it to the zipper pull; tension it so the pull is held against the end stop. The latch + tension prevents vibration-driven slider creep.

A: Propose a way to improve collaboration between research and engineering teams. Constraints: no new meetings; must reduce rework.

B.rule: Treat handoff as interface definition with testable contracts; control knobs are minimal viable interface (MVI), contract tests, and change notifications tied to breaking changes.
B.unlocks: Reduce rework by aligning on executable interfaces rather than prose.
B.justification: Rework is caused by silent interface mismatches; executable contracts detect mismatch early and cheaply.

C: Define an MVI: one input format, one output schema, and one metric function. Encode them as contract tests (sample inputs + expected outputs/shape) committed in the repo. Any change that breaks the contract must update the test and include a migration note. This makes alignment automatic and prevents late rework.

A: Design a method to keep a candle flame steady in a drafty room. Constraints: only a glass and aluminum foil.

B.rule: Treat flicker as airflow turbulence at the flame; control knobs are wind shielding (glass), flow shaping (foil baffle), and oxygen feed path (controlled inlet).
B.unlocks: Create a low-turbulence micro-environment for stable combustion.
B.justification: Flames destabilize when turbulent eddies hit them; shielding and controlled inlets smooth airflow while maintaining oxygen.

C: Place the candle inside the glass to block direct drafts. Use foil to form a partial collar at the glass opening that leaves a small notch as a controlled air inlet. The glass dampens turbulence and the notch stabilizes airflow direction, reducing flicker.

A: Propose a way to stop people from cutting in line at a popular café pickup counter. Constraints: no extra staff; must stay friendly.

B.rule: Treat cutting as ambiguity in queue state; control knobs are state visibility (who’s next), claim tokens (proof of turn), and spatial affordances that make the correct behavior easiest.
B.unlocks: A self-enforcing queue via visible tokens and clear spatial mapping.
B.justification: People cut when the system doesn’t clearly encode order; tokens and spatial mapping eliminate ambiguity without confrontation.

C: Use numbered claim tokens at ordering (paper slips). Create a single visible “Now Serving: #__” board near pickup and a marked waiting zone aligned to token order. Staff only updates the number; customers self-sort. Visibility + tokens reduces cutting without extra staff.

A: Design a method to keep a small plant alive for 10 days while traveling. Constraints: no auto-watering devices; only a bottle, string, and a towel.
D: Water it a lot before leaving

B.rule: Treat watering as capillary dosing; control knobs are wick geometry (string/towel), reservoir height (bottle), and evaporation reduction (shade/towel cover).
B.unlocks: A passive drip system with stable flow rate.
B.justification: Capillary action can meter water from a reservoir; controlling height and wick thickness stabilizes dosing.

C: Fill the bottle, place it slightly above the soil level, and run a string (or towel strip) as a wick with one end buried a few cm into the soil and the other submerged in the bottle. Move the plant to shade and drape part of the towel loosely around the pot to reduce evaporation. The wick meters water slowly for days.

A: Propose a way to reduce confusion in a multi-step user onboarding flow. Constraints: cannot remove steps; must keep same screens.

B.rule: Treat onboarding as a state machine; control knobs are state visibility (what step you’re in), preconditions (what you need before continuing), and error locality (detect errors at the step they occur).
B.unlocks: Reduce confusion by making state and preconditions explicit and errors local, without changing screens.
B.justification: Users get lost when state is hidden and errors appear later; clear state and local validation prevent misattribution.

C: Add a persistent “You are here” step indicator with one-sentence goal per step, and list exactly one precondition at the top of each screen (“Have your ID ready”, “Connect Wi-Fi”). Validate inputs immediately on each screen with a clear fix message. Same screens, but clearer state + preconditions + local errors reduce confusion.

A: Design a way to prevent sunscreen from getting into your eyes while sweating. Constraints: no new products; only a towel and water.

B.rule: Treat eye-sting as fluid transport: sweat carries dissolved sunscreen along gravity paths; control knobs are barrier placement (towel headband), dilution/rinse timing (water), and application geometry (keep off brow ridge).
B.unlocks: Interrupt the transport path before it reaches the eyes.
B.justification: Most sting is sunscreen migrating from forehead; adding an absorbent barrier and rinsing early prevents downstream flow.

C: Fold the towel into a thin headband and place it just above the eyebrows to absorb sweat before it runs down. Rinse forehead with a small amount of water early (before heavy sweat) and blot with the towel to remove migrating residue. Keep sunscreen off the eyebrow ridge when applying (leave a small gap).

A: Propose a method to reduce false positives in an anomaly detection system without missing real anomalies. Constraints: limited labels; must be deployable quickly.

B.rule: Treat alerts as hypotheses with cheap refutation tests; control knobs are context conditioning (what ‘normal’ means per segment), calibration (probability mapping), and two-stage gating (fast filter then expensive check).
B.unlocks: Cut false positives by adding segment-aware calibration and a lightweight verification stage.
B.justification: Most false positives come from unmodeled context; conditioning and a second gate reject spurious cases while preserving sensitivity.

C: Segment alerts by context (time-of-day, device type, location bucket) and calibrate thresholds per segment using a small labeled set plus historical stable periods. Add a second-stage verifier that checks simple invariants (consistency across adjacent time windows, cross-sensor agreement). Only promote alerts that pass the verifier. Context calibration + two-stage gating reduces false positives quickly.

A: Design a low-cost way to keep a bike seat dry during sudden rain. Constraints: only a plastic bag and a rubber band.

B.rule: Treat wetness as surface exposure + pooling; control knobs are occlusion (bag cover), edge sealing (band), and drainage geometry (avoid pockets).
B.unlocks: Create a sealed, pocket-free cover that sheds water instead of collecting it.
B.justification: Seats get wet from direct rainfall and pooling; sealing edges and removing folds prevents trapped water.

C: Pull the plastic bag tightly over the seat so the top is smooth with no folds, then secure it underneath the seat with the rubber band. Ensure the bag slopes downward so water runs off rather than pooling. Tight cover + sealed edge + no pockets keeps the seat dry.

A: Propose a way to make brainstorming sessions produce fewer generic ideas. Constraints: no extra time; same group.
D: Tell people to be more creative

B.rule: Treat idea generation as search in a landscape with local minima; control knobs are constraint switching (change what’s held fixed), distance jumps (forced analogies), and anti-duplication pressure (ban near-neighbors).
B.unlocks: Force exploration away from obvious basins without adding time.
B.justification: Groups converge on easy regions; structured jumps and near-neighbor bans push the search into new regions fast.

C: Run 3 rounds of 5 minutes: each round changes one constraint (budget unlimited → time unlimited → legal constraints strict). In each round, the first 3 ideas are banned (“no near-neighbors”), and each person must use a forced analogy domain (kitchen, sports, biology). Constraint switching + analogy jumps + near-neighbor ban reduces generic clustering.

A: Design a method to keep a pan from sliding on a smooth countertop while stirring. Constraints: only a towel; must be quick.
D: Hold the pan with your other hand

B.rule: Treat sliding as insufficient friction under lateral force; control knobs are normal force distribution, friction coefficient (towel), and contact area.
B.unlocks: Create a high-friction interface to raise static friction above stirring shear.
B.justification: A towel increases real contact area and friction; distributing load prevents small contact points from slipping.

C: Fold the towel into a thick pad and place it under the pan base. Press down once to seat it so the towel fibers grip the countertop. The increased friction and larger contact area stop sliding while you stir.

A: Propose a way to ensure LLM tool-calls remain safe and aligned in production. Constraints: cannot rely on manual review.

B.rule: Treat tool-use as a policy with enforceable pre/post-conditions; control knobs are capability scoping (least privilege), invariant checks (what must always hold), and reversible execution (dry-run then commit).
B.unlocks: Automatic safety by turning actions into checked transactions.
B.justification: Risk comes from irreversible side effects; transactions with invariants prevent unsafe actions without human review.

C: Wrap each tool call as a transaction: model must first produce an explicit intent + parameters; the system runs a dry-run validator against invariants (scope, rate, PII, allowed targets). Only then is the call executed; post-conditions are verified (expected diffs only), otherwise auto-rollback. Least privilege + invariants + dry-run/commit yields scalable safety.

A: Design a way to reduce wasted time searching for files in a shared drive. Constraints: no new tools; must work with existing folders.

B.rule: Treat the drive as a library system; control knobs are a single canonical index (where to look first), deterministic naming for recent items, and an expiration policy for temporary drafts.
B.unlocks: Reduce search by making the first lookup location and naming scheme predictable.
B.justification: Search time drops when there’s a stable ‘front door’ and predictable naming for the most-used artifacts.

C: Create a single top-level “INDEX” folder with one page per project linking to canonical locations (not copies). Enforce a naming rule for active docs: YYYY-MM-DD_topic_owner_v#. Drafts older than 30 days must move to /Archive or be deleted. Front-door index + deterministic naming + expiry reduces hunting without new tools.

A: Propose a method to keep a group focused during a 45-minute workshop with mixed seniority. Constraints: no facilitator interventions once it starts.
D: Ask people to stay on topic

B.rule: Treat the session as a self-running program; control knobs are pre-committed phases, local rules per phase, and a visible state transition trigger that doesn’t require facilitation.
B.unlocks: A ‘mechanical’ workshop that progresses via built-in phase rules.
B.justification: Workshops derail when state transitions depend on social dominance; pre-committed phases + triggers keep flow stable.

C: Pre-print (or pre-write) a 3-phase script on one slide/board: 15 min generate (silent write), 15 min cluster (move notes), 15 min decide (dot-vote + pick top 2). Use a single visible trigger: when music/phone timer ends, everyone must switch phases automatically (no discussion required). Phase script + local rules + automatic trigger keeps focus without facilitator interventions.

A: Design a way to prevent forgetting items when leaving home. Constraints: no apps/alarms; must take <30 seconds/day.

B.rule: Treat leaving as a mandatory state transition through a single choke point; control knobs are physical gating (block the path), cue specificity (one cue per missing item), and self-reset (cue disappears when done).
B.unlocks: A forcing function that makes forgetting mechanically hard, not cognitively unlikely.
B.justification: People fail at recall; forcing functions convert ‘remember’ into ‘cannot proceed until resolved’ with minimal effort.

C: Pick one choke point (doorknob/shoes/keys). For each critical item, use a physical gate: place the item on top of your shoes or hook keys through the item’s strap so you must touch it to leave. After grabbing it, the gate self-resets because the item is now with you. One choke point + forcing function eliminates most forgetting in seconds.

A: Propose a way to reduce interruptions from teammates while staying approachable. Constraints: no new tools; must not feel rude.

B.rule: Treat interruptions as queue arrivals; control knobs are triage bandwidth (a small intake window), visible service levels (when you’ll respond), and a low-friction dropbox for requests.
B.unlocks: Convert random walk-ups into batched, predictable intake without reducing helpfulness.
B.justification: Interruptions hurt when arrivals are unbounded; a visible intake policy preserves access while protecting deep work.

C: Create two daily 10-minute “intake windows” and make them visible (e.g., a small desk sign: “Questions: 11:50 & 16:50”). Outside windows, ask people to leave a one-line request on a shared note or message thread with a required tag (BLOCKER/NONBLOCKER). You stay approachable (guaranteed windows) while batching arrivals.

A: Design a low-cost way to keep a drink from spilling while walking. Constraints: only a rubber band and a napkin.

B.rule: Treat spills as slosh resonance; control knobs are free-surface damping (napkin), aperture control (partial cover), and excitation reduction (hand coupling).
B.unlocks: Add damping and reduce slosh amplitude without changing the cup.
B.justification: Slosh grows when the liquid resonates; damping and partial covering break wave formation and reduce splashing.

C: Twist the napkin into a loose ring and place it at the rim as a partial cover (leaving a sip gap). Use the rubber band to hold the napkin ring in place. The napkin absorbs small waves and breaks the free surface, reducing slosh and spills while walking.

A: Propose a way to stop recurring misalignment between product requirements and engineering implementation. Constraints: no extra meetings; must be async.

B.rule: Treat requirements as testable invariants; control knobs are executable examples, acceptance tests, and change tracking when invariants evolve.
B.unlocks: Alignment through shared, runnable “truth objects” instead of prose interpretation.
B.justification: Misalignment happens when requirements are ambiguous; invariants expressed as tests remove ambiguity and detect drift.

C: For every requirement, provide 3 executable examples (input → expected behavior) and 1 invariant (“must always hold”). Engineers implement until those examples pass. Any requirement change must update the examples first (change tracking). This keeps alignment without meetings because the tests are the contract.

A: Design a way to reduce queue time at a single coffee machine in an office. Constraints: no buying another machine; no policing.
D: Teach people how to use it

B.rule: Treat the machine as a bottleneck with setup-time overhead; control knobs are setup batching (group similar drinks), pre-staging (inputs ready), and fast/slow lane separation.
B.unlocks: Reduce variability and setup time at the bottleneck without enforcement.
B.justification: Queues blow up from high service-time variance; batching and pre-staging reduce average time and variance.

C: Add a pre-staging shelf: cups, sugar, stirrers, and milk placed before the machine so the machine time is only “brew.” Label two physical lanes: quick-brew (espresso/americano) and custom (milk foam/syrup). People self-select because the custom lane waits longer. Pre-staging + lane separation reduces average service time and variance.

A: Propose a method to help someone stop procrastinating on a single important task. Constraints: no apps; must start in 2 minutes.

B.rule: Treat procrastination as initiation friction; control knobs are activation energy (first step size), commitment device (tiny irreversible start), and immediate feedback.
B.unlocks: Start by making the first move trivially small and slightly irreversible.
B.justification: The main barrier is starting; once momentum exists, continuation costs drop dramatically.

C: Do a 2-minute “irreversible micro-start”: open the document/tool and write a single ugly placeholder sentence or run a single command that creates an output file. Then set a 5-minute “keep the streak” rule: you may stop after 5 minutes, but you must leave a visible next-action marker (one bullet) in the doc. Tiny start + slight irreversibility + immediate artifact breaks the stall.

A: Design a low-cost method to keep a child’s hands clean during a long outdoor snack. Constraints: only water and a towel; no soap.
D: Tell them not to touch dirt

B.rule: Treat mess as transfer at contact events; control knobs are contact frequency (designated clean hand), barrier layer (towel), and rinse scheduling (micro-rinses before buildup).
B.unlocks: Reduce transfer by creating a clean-hand protocol with periodic resets.
B.justification: Dirt spreads through repeated transfers; separating roles and resetting early prevents compounding.

C: Create a “clean hand / dirty hand” rule: one hand holds food, the other touches surfaces. Keep the towel as a barrier on the food-holding hand between bites (wrap fingers lightly). Do quick micro-rinses: splash and towel-blot every 10–15 minutes before stickiness accumulates. Role separation + barrier + early resets keeps hands much cleaner.

A: Propose a way to reduce errors in repeated manual data entry in spreadsheets. Constraints: cannot automate with scripts; must be procedural.

B.rule: Treat entry as a noisy channel; control knobs are chunking (units), redundancy (check digits/echo), and immediate local validation (spot checks per chunk).
B.unlocks: Catch errors early by forcing structure and redundancy without automation.
B.justification: Most errors survive because they aren’t detectable until the end; structured chunking plus redundancy makes them visible immediately.

C: Enter in fixed chunks (e.g., 5 rows at a time). After each chunk, do a “checksum echo”: compute a simple sum/count for that chunk and compare to the source (or expected total). Only then proceed. If mismatch, the error is in the last chunk, so you search a small space. Chunking + redundancy + immediate validation cuts error rate dramatically.

A: Design a method to keep a small room brighter without adding new lights. Constraints: only a mirror and moving furniture.

B.rule: Treat light as rays with losses; control knobs are redirecting high-intensity rays (mirror), reducing occlusion (furniture placement), and increasing effective path coverage (bounce geometry).
B.unlocks: Increase perceived brightness by re-aiming existing light into dark regions.
B.justification: Rooms feel dim when bright rays hit low-value surfaces or get blocked; redirecting and unblocking raises usable illumination.

C: Place the mirror so it reflects the brightest source (window or main lamp) onto the darkest wall/ceiling area (not into eyes). Move tall furniture away from the light path and keep reflective surfaces (mirror) facing into the room. Ray redirection + reduced occlusion spreads existing light across more of the room.

A: Propose a way to reduce accidental production incidents from config changes. Constraints: no new tooling; teams move fast.

B.rule: Treat configs as a high-impact control surface; control knobs are blast radius (scope), progressive rollout (phased exposure), and reversible commit (canary + auto-revert).
B.unlocks: Safety via staged exposure and fast reversibility without slowing all work.
B.justification: Incidents happen when changes hit 100% instantly; staged rollouts localize risk and enable quick rollback.

C: Adopt a config rule: every change must include a scope parameter (single region/service/tenant first) and a 2-step rollout (10% then 100%) with a fixed observation window. If error metrics exceed a threshold, revert immediately. This uses blast radius control + progressive rollout + reversibility using existing deployment habits.

A: Design a way to keep a paper stack from blowing away outdoors. Constraints: only a rock and a rubber band.

B.rule: Treat wind loss as uplift plus sliding; control knobs are normal force (rock), shear resistance (rubber band friction), and edge flutter reduction (bind edges).
B.unlocks: Stabilize both lift and shear with minimal materials.
B.justification: Paper flies when edges flutter and lift reduces friction; binding edges and adding mass prevents both failure modes.

C: Wrap the rubber band around the stack near one edge (creating a tight ‘spine’) to stop edge flutter. Place the rock on the banded area so the rock presses the high-friction band and increases normal force. The band reduces flutter; the rock prevents sliding and lift.

A: Propose a way to improve reliability of a daily data pipeline that sometimes produces wrong results silently. Constraints: no new monitoring services.

B.rule: Treat correctness as invariants over transformations; control knobs are sentinel checks (cheap invariants), reconciliation (compare two independent computations), and quarantine (don’t publish until checks pass).
B.unlocks: Detect silent wrongness by forcing contradictions to appear before publishing.
B.justification: Silent failures persist when only one computation exists; invariants and reconciliation expose drift without extra infra.

C: Add a “sentinel rowset” that flows through the pipeline daily and must produce fixed outputs (hash, counts, key sums). In parallel, compute one key metric two ways (e.g., aggregation via two groupings) and compare. If any check fails, quarantine outputs (don’t publish) and emit a single failure report in existing logs. Sentinels + reconciliation + quarantine prevents silent bad publishes.

A: Design a method to prevent a candle from tipping over on an uneven surface. Constraints: only aluminum foil and a glass.
D: Move it to a flat surface

B.rule: Treat tipping as center-of-mass leaving support polygon; control knobs are base widening (foil flange), containment (glass), and friction increase.
B.unlocks: Increase stability by enlarging the support area and adding a containment wall.
B.justification: A wider base raises the tipping threshold; containment prevents lateral motion that initiates tipping.

C: Form a wide foil ‘flange’ under the candle by folding foil into a flat disk larger than the candle base, then place the candle and foil disk inside the glass. The foil widens the effective base and adds friction; the glass contains lateral movement, preventing tipping.

A: Propose a way to keep conversations from derailing in a conflict discussion between partners. Constraints: no therapist; must be usable tonight.

B.rule: Treat conflict as bandwidth collapse under emotional load; control knobs are turn-taking (one speaker), acknowledgment packets (reflect back), and congestion control (pause triggers).
B.unlocks: Keep the channel stable by enforcing simple communication protocol rules.
B.justification: Derailment happens when both sides transmit simultaneously and meaning isn’t acknowledged; protocols prevent overload.

C: Use a strict 3-step loop: (1) one person speaks for 60 seconds, (2) the other must reflect back the core claim in one sentence (“What I hear is…”), (3) only then can they respond for 60 seconds. If either raises voice or repeats a point twice, trigger a 2-minute silent pause (congestion control). Turn-taking + acknowledgment + pauses keeps the channel from collapsing.

A: Design a way to keep a phone from sliding on a car dashboard. Constraints: only a towel and a rubber band.
D: Put it in a cup holder

B.rule: Treat sliding as insufficient friction and poor constraint geometry; control knobs are friction layer (towel), mechanical stop (band), and incline direction.
B.unlocks: Create a high-friction base and a simple physical stop to prevent drift.
B.justification: Friction alone may fail on bumps; adding a stop converts sliding into blocked motion.

C: Fold the towel into a small pad under the phone to increase friction. Use the rubber band to create a ‘bumper’: loop it around a fixed dashboard feature (or the towel bundle itself) so it forms a ridge that the phone cannot cross. Friction + stop prevents sliding during turns and braking.

A: Propose a way to reduce decision fatigue in a team that makes many small choices daily. Constraints: must not reduce autonomy.

B.rule: Treat small decisions as cache misses; control knobs are default policies (cache hits), exception routing (only hard cases), and periodic cache refresh (update defaults).
B.unlocks: Preserve autonomy by letting people override defaults, but reduce fatigue by making defaults available.
B.justification: Fatigue comes from repeated deliberation on similar cases; strong defaults turn repeats into quick ‘yes’ decisions.

C: Create a lightweight “default library” for common choices (PR size, metric thresholds, naming, escalation). Teams start from defaults automatically; any deviation must state a one-line reason. Review defaults weekly for 5 minutes async and update only when exceptions recur. Defaults + exception routing + refresh reduces fatigue without removing autonomy.

A: Design a way to keep a sandwich from getting crushed in a bag. Constraints: only two index cards and a rubber band.
D: Put it in a hard container

B.rule: Treat crushing as compressive load transmission; control knobs are creating a stiff sandwich panel (cards), load path rerouting (band), and buckling resistance.
B.unlocks: Build a lightweight structural shell that takes the load instead of the food.
B.justification: A thin stiff panel can carry bending loads; bracing both sides prevents direct compression.

C: Place the sandwich between two index cards (top and bottom) to form a stiff panel sandwich. Wrap a rubber band around the cards to keep them aligned. Heavy items press on the cards, which distribute load and resist bending, keeping the sandwich intact.

A: Propose a way to reduce late arrival to morning meetings across a team. Constraints: no penalties; no reminders.
D: Ask people to be on time

B.rule: Treat punctuality as a coordination equilibrium; control knobs are immediate cost of lateness (without punishment), early payoff (reward for on-time), and meeting value density (front-load value).
B.unlocks: Shift incentives by making the first minutes uniquely valuable and late minutes less so.
B.justification: People are late when they believe nothing critical happens early; changing payoff structure changes behavior.

C: Front-load the only high-value segment: the first 4 minutes are “decision lock-in” where owners confirm today’s commitments and pick the top blocker. After minute 4, no new decisions are made—only clarifications. People show up for the high-value early payoff, without penalties or reminders.

A: Design a method to keep a cold drink from sweating onto a wooden table. Constraints: only a paper towel and a rubber band.

B.rule: Treat condensation as a liquid collection and routing problem; control knobs are absorbent capture (paper towel), drip path control (banded collar), and contact isolation.
B.unlocks: Capture water before it reaches the table by creating a dedicated absorbent ring.
B.justification: Sweat damages wood when droplets reach the surface; intercepting and absorbing at the cup prevents contact.

C: Twist the paper towel into a thick ring and place it around the base as a “moat.” Use the rubber band to hold the ring snug against the cup so condensation wicks into the towel rather than dripping outward. This intercepts and absorbs sweat before it hits the table.

A: Propose a way to reduce misclicks on small UI buttons on mobile. Constraints: can’t redesign layout; only interaction behavior changes allowed.

B.rule: Treat taps as noisy measurements of intent; control knobs are temporal filtering (tap-and-hold), redundancy (gesture confirmation only for risky actions), and error correction (easy undo).
B.unlocks: Reduce errors by adding lightweight intent verification and fast correction without layout change.
B.justification: You can’t change targets, but you can change the decoding of intent and provide cheap recovery.

C: For risky actions only, require a 200ms hold (temporal filter) instead of a single tap. For non-risky actions, keep normal taps. Add a 3-second “undo” snackbar for all actions. Filtering + selective redundancy + easy correction cuts misclick harm without layout changes.

A: Design a way to keep a towel from slipping off a hook. Constraints: only a rubber band and a paperclip.

B.rule: Treat slipping as low friction plus poor constraint; control knobs are adding a mechanical latch (paperclip) and increasing normal force (band) at the hook interface.
B.unlocks: Convert a friction-only hang into a latched hang.
B.justification: Friction fails when the towel shifts; a latch creates a geometric constraint that prevents drop.

C: Fold a small corner of the towel into a tight loop, clip it with the paperclip to create a rigid eyelet, then put that eyelet on the hook. Wrap the rubber band around the hook and the paperclip once to increase normal force and prevent the eyelet from bouncing off. Latch + added force stops slipping.

A: Propose a way to keep a shared calendar accurate when people forget to update it. Constraints: no enforcement; must be lightweight.

B.rule: Treat the calendar as a source-of-truth ledger that must be updated at the moment of commitment; control knobs are commitment coupling (update is part of the action), friction (make update easiest path), and reconciliation (periodic drift correction).
B.unlocks: Make updating automatic by tying it to commitment moments, not memory later.
B.justification: People forget after the fact; coupling updates to the commitment event prevents drift.

C: Adopt a rule: any commitment that affects time must be made only by creating/updating the calendar event (not by chat). If it’s agreed in chat, the final message must be the event link. Once per week, do a 5-minute “reconciliation sweep” where each person checks only tomorrow’s events for drift. Commitment coupling + small reconciliation keeps accuracy lightweight.

A: Design a method to keep glasses from fogging when entering an air-conditioned building from humid outdoors. Constraints: only soap and a cloth.

B.rule: Treat fogging as condensation nucleation; control knobs are surface wetting (surfactant film), droplet coalescence (sheeting), and initial lens temperature (reduce gradient).
B.unlocks: Prevent droplets from forming by making water spread into a thin transparent film.
B.justification: Surfactants lower surface tension so condensation forms a uniform layer rather than scattering droplets.

C: Rub a tiny amount of soap onto both lens surfaces, then buff until invisible with the cloth, leaving a thin surfactant film. When you enter AC, condensation forms as a transparent sheet instead of droplets, reducing fogging significantly.
